EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men were stranded on a sand bar in Tomales Bay on the 9th as the tide rose around them. They tried to swim back to shore in cold rough waters but one man drowned. The other was able to be rescued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men were stranded on a sand bar in Tomales Bay on the 9th as the tide rose around them. They tried to swim back to shore in cold rough waters but one man drowned. The other was able to be rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 44 year old man died the afternoon of the 21st when his hang glider landed in the ocean near Devil's Slide along the San Mateo coastline. The pilot landed in the ocean and unhooked himself from the glider but could not make it to shore due to heavy surf and cliffs. He was found unconscious and not breathing and was pulled from the water. CPR was performed but the victim was not responsive and pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact mini atmospheric river impacted Southern California January 14th and 15th which brought heavy rain and snow to the region. Seal Beach reported 2 inches of rain in 2 hours, which caused extensive flash flooding. Water was up to doorways outside of homes, and the Pacific Coast Highway was closed for over a day in Huntington Beach due to flooding. Rainfall rates remained below flash flood thresholds at the Holy and Cranston burn scars during this mini-AR event. Dense fog was reported in the Cajon Pass on the morning of January 16th, which resulted in a 19-car pileup and 35 patients with minor to moderate injuries. ||A larger atmospheric river moved into Southern California on January 17th, bringing heavy rain and snow to the region. Extensive flash flooding occurred due to the heavy rain. Swift water rescues occurred on the Santa Ana River in Riverside due to flooding. Rainfall rates exceeded flash flooding thresholds for the Holy fire burn scar on the 17th.||Overall, 6 to 12 inches of rain fell in the Santa Ana and San Bernardino County Mountains between January 14-17. Snowfall amounts in the mountains ranged from 4 to 12 inches above 6500 ft. Major surf and coastal flooding occurred during this event as well, which resulted in extensive damage of the Ocean Beach Pier in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding from heavy rainfall over Holy fire scar in Trilogy parkway and Glen Eden with water going around homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Critical fire weather conditions were forecast for Oct 23-24 and again on October 27-28 with the eastern Pacific High allowing trough to slide into the Great Basin/Nevada areas. This caused the pressure gradients to tighten over the county warning area, which lead to additional rounds of strong northerly and easterly winds. With these winds forecast, dry conditions still in place, and fuels still being primed a Red Flag Warning was issued for most of interior northern California. Fortunately no major wildfires resulted from this but utility power lines did suffer from wind and tree damage across much of northern California. Time stamps for events are based on strongest wind gusts that occurred between Oct 23-38.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station, Knoxville Creek, near Hidden Valley Creek reported a non-thunderstorm related wind gust up to 62 mph. Throughout Lake County, other mesonet stations reported peak wind gusts of 30-53 mph. The local utility company for Lake County reported tree and wind related damage to power lines near the Highlands, Konocti, Lucerne, Redbud, and Clear Lake areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moved through Southern California on January 31st and brought heavy rain and thunderstorms to the region. Thunderstorms brought occasional heavy precipitation, resulting in flash flooding. The coastal areas of Orange and San Diego County received a half an inch of precipitation over 2 hours. The Pacific Coast Highway (Highway 1) was closed due to flooding. Lightning struck a tree and damaged a fence in San Diego County. 40 cloud-to-ground strikes were observed in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CalTrans reported Pacific Coast Highway (highway 1) closed due to flooding. This area received around 0.5 inch of rainfall in 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inside slider low moved into Southern California on January 21 and brought widespread precipitation and strong westerly winds to the region. A period of Santa Ana winds immediately followed the inside slider, and brought high winds to the region, especially in the mountain areas. The peak observed wind gust was 84 knots in Burns Canyon. Two people were killed when a large tree fell on their home in Point Loma in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Borrego Air Ranch Airport mesonet reported a peak wind gust of 54 knots between 0600 and 0700 PST on January 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Monterey local 18 year old fell off a cliff at Garrapata State Park Saturday morning January 12 while hiking. After a few crashes of the waves his friends were unable to locate him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Monterey local 18-year old man fell off a cliff at Garrapata State Park around 11:44 Saturday morning and into the Pacific Ocean. According to Coast Guard spokeswoman Emily Rowan, the 18-year old fell off a cliff at Garrapata Beach while hiking with two friends. According to the search report, the two friends initially saw the man put a thumbs up after he fell, but after a few crashes of the waves they were unable to locate him. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High tides and surf brought large waves and coastal flooding to Southern California. Areas of the San Diego County coastline observed sets as high as 15 ft and significant coastal flooding due to the King Tides. Surf was less extreme in Orange County but received significant coastal flooding during this event. Many water rescues due to the high surf and rip currents, and extensive damage to the Ocean Beach Pier in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Diego lifeguards reported sets as high as 15 ft during this 3 day period of high surf. In Ocean Beach, sets were as high as 10 ft, and the pier sustained extensive damage. The railings were broken on both sides of the pier, and it had to be closed down for an extended period of time for repairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Humidity was high in the low levels of the atmosphere on January 7th, winds were light and skies were generally clear. These conditions set the stage for the formation of fog, and indeed, patchy dense fog developed across portions of the Imperial valley during the morning hours. At about 0800PST a member of the public reported visibility near 1/4 mile about 1 mile northwest of the town of Imperial. A Dense Fog Advisory was in effect at the time of the report. Fortunately there were no reports of accidents due to the sharply restricted visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High low level humidity, light winds and generally clear skies provided optimal conditions for the formation of fog, and indeed, patchy dense fog developed across portions of the Imperial Valley during the morning hours on January 7th. At 0710PST a member of the public reported that visibility had fallen to 1/4 mile in dense fog about 1 mile northwest of Imperial. A Dense Fog Advisory was in effect at the time of the report. No accidents were reported due to the sharply reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty southwest winds developed across portions of Imperial County during the morning hours on January 21st. The winds were not severe, but they were in excess of 40 mph and sufficient to warrant the issuance of Wind Advisories for favored areas in far southeast California. Gusty winds in excess of 40 mph managed to down a telephone pole near the town of Calexico, according to local law enforcement. No injuries were reported due to the falling telephone pole. Calexico was located well to the south of Imperial and just north of the Mexican border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty southwest winds developed across portions of far southeast California during the morning hours on January 21, and some of the stronger gusts affected the south portions of the Imperial Valley. According to local law enforcement, at 0700PST wind gusts estimated as high as 50 mph blew down a telephone pole near the town of Calexico. No injuries were reported due to the falling pole. A Wind Advisory was in effect for portions of far southeast California at the time but the advisory did not include the Imperial Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system pushed into northern California during the morning of January 5. This system pushed a precipitation band through central California during the afternoon and evening of January 5 which produced a quarter to half of an inch of rain across much of the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County Mountains and half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation across the southern Sierra Nevada and an estimated 12 to 18 inches of snow fell above 7000 feet from SNOTEL observations. The Kern County Deserts were rain shadowed for much of this event and most areas only picked up a few hundredths of an inch of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tenaya Lake SNOTEL picked up an estimated 15 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a deep moisture fetch pushed into central California during the afternoon of January 16 and brought moderate to heavy precipitation along with strong winds to much of the area through the afternoon of January 17. Several reports of roadway flooding were received during the morning of January 17 when the heaviest precipitation occurred. Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in the Ferguson Fire burn area in Mariposa County and State Route 140 was closed for over 11 hours. The precipitation turned to showers and isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of January 17. One thunderstorm produced a tornado east of Clovis which was rated as EF-1 following a storm survey of the damage it produced. Most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between half an inch to an inch of rain while the Southern Sierra Nevada had 2 to 4 inches of liquid precipitation and the Kern County Mountains had 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet where several locations in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada above 7000 feet picked up 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall. There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crabtree Meadow SNOTEL (10700 feet) picked up 14.0 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front swept through California on January 5th bringing widespread rainfall and gusty winds. Unstable air behind the frontal passage and sufficient low level shear allowed shallow thunderstorms to develop over the coastal waters, some of which contained rotating cells. Two waterspouts developed and made landfall as tornadoes on the 6th. Additionally, heavy rainfall and strong winds caused roadway flooding, minor debris flows, and numerous downed trees across the region. This storm system caused two fatalities; one caused by a downed tree in Berkeley and another man died in Santa Rosa Creek due to rising waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet on Highland Peak Road measured gust of 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate to strong atmospheric river impacted much of California in the middle of the month. A weak surface low developed off the coast on January 15th bringing moderate to heavy rainfall to portions of the region. Over the next 24 to 36 hours a second strong low pressure system moved to the north and east bringing heavy rain, destructive winds, high surf, flooding, and thunderstorms to the Bay Area. Numerous reports were received of downed trees and power lines. Winds were recorded between 60 and 100 mph. Downed trees resulted in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding at I280 N and Mariposa St off ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave ahead of an upper low pushed into the Sierra early on the 15th. This shortwave produced moderate to heavy snow in the Sierra Nevada and northeast California through the morning hours of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 17.5 and 24 inches fell near and west of California Highway 89 between the 15th and the morning of the 16th, with 29 inches falling above 8000 feet MSL near Squaw Valley Ski Resort. Below 7000 feet MSL east of California Highway 89 and south of Interstate 80, between 11.5 and 23 inches of snow fell. South of US Highway 50, 10 inches of snow fell at 2 miles northwest of Meyers and 7 inches of snow fell at Heavenly Ski Resort. Westbound Interstate 80 was closed at the Nevada state line due to an avalanche between 2324PST and 2339PST on the 15th according to the California Department of Transportation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a series of winter storms moved through central California from the afternoon of January 6 through the early morning of January 7. While this storm produced another round of rain and higher elevation snow across the area with many locations in the southern Sierra Nevada picking up between 1 to 2 inches of liquid precipitation, the main impact from this storm was strong winds were most noticed across the San Joaquin Valley where several trees and power lines were blown down or knocked over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree blocking the eastbound lane at West Bellevue Road and State Route 59 5NNW Merced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure pushed inland into California during the morning of January 19 following the departure of the powerful storm system that brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds to the area on January 17. Skies cleared out and winds diminished across the San Joaquin Valley from Fresno southward during the morning of January 19 which allowed for areas of dense fog to form toward daybreak. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through central California during the evening of January 20 early morning of January 21 bringing a brief period of moderate to locally heavy precipitation along with strong wind gusts. Several reports of damaging winds were received during this time frame as winds downed several trees and power poles in the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts above 35 mph. There were also several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts and some low impact indicator sites measured gusts between 75 and 90 mph. Rainfall amounts varied in the San Joaquin Valley as some locations in the east central valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch while most of the west side of the valley was rain shadowed and did not pick up much rain. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between a half inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation while several locations above 7000 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of beneficial snowfall. The precipitation ended by the evening of January 21 as the frontal system moved out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Ponderosa Basin reported 1.03 inches of rainfall in 12 hours which mainly fell in a short period overnight. The rain changed over to snow toward sunrise and a light dusting was on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through central California during the evening of January 20 early morning of January 21 bringing a brief period of moderate to locally heavy precipitation along with strong wind gusts. Several reports of damaging winds were received during this time frame as winds downed several trees and power poles in the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts above 35 mph. There were also several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts and some low impact indicator sites measured gusts between 75 and 90 mph. Rainfall amounts varied in the San Joaquin Valley as some locations in the east central valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch while most of the west side of the valley was rain shadowed and did not pick up much rain. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between a half inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation while several locations above 7000 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of beneficial snowfall. The precipitation ended by the evening of January 21 as the frontal system moved out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree down on power and phone wires with several branches blocking a roadway in north Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front swept through California on January 5th bringing widespread rainfall and gusty winds. Unstable air behind the frontal passage and sufficient low level shear allowed shallow thunderstorms to develop over the coastal waters, some of which contained rotating cells. Two waterspouts developed and made landfall as tornadoes on the 6th. Additionally, heavy rainfall and strong winds caused roadway flooding, minor debris flows, and numerous downed trees across the region. This storm system caused two fatalities; one caused by a downed tree in Berkeley and another man died in Santa Rosa Creek due to rising waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees showed indications of small and large limb damage along the ridge line. One tray may have been topped. Estimated peak winds of 65 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a deep moisture fetch pushed into central California during the afternoon of January 16 and brought moderate to heavy precipitation along with strong winds to much of the area through the afternoon of January 17. Several reports of roadway flooding were received during the morning of January 17 when the heaviest precipitation occurred. Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in the Ferguson Fire burn area in Mariposa County and State Route 140 was closed for over 11 hours. The precipitation turned to showers and isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of January 17. One thunderstorm produced a tornado east of Clovis which was rated as EF-1 following a storm survey of the damage it produced. Most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between half an inch to an inch of rain while the Southern Sierra Nevada had 2 to 4 inches of liquid precipitation and the Kern County Mountains had 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet where several locations in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada above 7000 feet picked up 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall. There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding affecting the westbound lanes of State Route 58 east of Cottonwood Road in Bakersfield.

There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported Redrock-Randsburg Road flooded at the intersection of Garlock Road near Randsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a deep moisture fetch pushed into central California during the afternoon of January 16 and brought moderate to heavy precipitation along with strong winds to much of the area through the afternoon of January 17. Several reports of roadway flooding were received during the morning of January 17 when the heaviest precipitation occurred. Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in the Ferguson Fire burn area in Mariposa County and State Route 140 was closed for over 11 hours. The precipitation turned to showers and isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of January 17. One thunderstorm produced a tornado east of Clovis which was rated as EF-1 following a storm survey of the damage it produced. Most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between half an inch to an inch of rain while the Southern Sierra Nevada had 2 to 4 inches of liquid precipitation and the Kern County Mountains had 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet where several locations in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada above 7000 feet picked up 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall. There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at the intersection of Pellisier Road and Banducci Road near Stallion Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through central California during the evening of January 20 early morning of January 21 bringing a brief period of moderate to locally heavy precipitation along with strong wind gusts. Several reports of damaging winds were received during this time frame as winds downed several trees and power poles in the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts above 35 mph. There were also several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts and some low impact indicator sites measured gusts between 75 and 90 mph. Rainfall amounts varied in the San Joaquin Valley as some locations in the east central valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch while most of the west side of the valley was rain shadowed and did not pick up much rain. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between a half inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation while several locations above 7000 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of beneficial snowfall. The precipitation ended by the evening of January 21 as the frontal system moved out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported trees down blocking the road 10 miles SSW of Shaver Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front swept through California on January 5th bringing widespread rainfall and gusty winds. Unstable air behind the frontal passage and sufficient low level shear allowed shallow thunderstorms to develop over the coastal waters, some of which contained rotating cells. Two waterspouts developed and made landfall as tornadoes on the 6th. Additionally, heavy rainfall and strong winds caused roadway flooding, minor debris flows, and numerous downed trees across the region. This storm system caused two fatalities; one caused by a downed tree in Berkeley and another man died in Santa Rosa Creek due to rising waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and flooding across Chalk Hill Road due to clogged drain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge pushed inland into California on the evening of January 9th and morning of January 10. As a result, skies cleared out and winds diminished across the San Joaquin Valley during the early morning hours which led to the development of widespread dense fog across much of the valley on the morning of the 10th and several locations reported visibility of an eighth of a mile or less. The dense fog was responsible for a fatal auto accident near Atwater. Several school districts in the valley opened late that morning. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning and dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several school districts either had delayed openings or delayed morning bus service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a deep moisture fetch pushed into central California during the afternoon of January 16 and brought moderate to heavy precipitation along with strong winds to much of the area through the afternoon of January 17. Several reports of roadway flooding were received during the morning of January 17 when the heaviest precipitation occurred. Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in the Ferguson Fire burn area in Mariposa County and State Route 140 was closed for over 11 hours. The precipitation turned to showers and isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of January 17. One thunderstorm produced a tornado east of Clovis which was rated as EF-1 following a storm survey of the damage it produced. Most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between half an inch to an inch of rain while the Southern Sierra Nevada had 2 to 4 inches of liquid precipitation and the Kern County Mountains had 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet where several locations in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada above 7000 feet picked up 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall. There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of roadway flooding on F St. in Bakersfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through central California during the evening of January 20 early morning of January 21 bringing a brief period of moderate to locally heavy precipitation along with strong wind gusts. Several reports of damaging winds were received during this time frame as winds downed several trees and power poles in the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts above 35 mph. There were also several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts and some low impact indicator sites measured gusts between 75 and 90 mph. Rainfall amounts varied in the San Joaquin Valley as some locations in the east central valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch while most of the west side of the valley was rain shadowed and did not pick up much rain. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between a half inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation while several locations above 7000 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of beneficial snowfall. The precipitation ended by the evening of January 21 as the frontal system moved out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported large trees blocking the roadway 3 miles NE of Squaw Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front swept through California on January 5th bringing widespread rainfall and gusty winds. Unstable air behind the frontal passage and sufficient low level shear allowed shallow thunderstorms to develop over the coastal waters, some of which contained rotating cells. Two waterspouts developed and made landfall as tornadoes on the 6th. Additionally, heavy rainfall and strong winds caused roadway flooding, minor debris flows, and numerous downed trees across the region. This storm system caused two fatalities; one caused by a downed tree in Berkeley and another man died in Santa Rosa Creek due to rising waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding at US 101N and S Airport Blvd off ramp, one foot of water in lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate to strong atmospheric river impacted much of California in the middle of the month. A weak surface low developed off the coast on January 15th bringing moderate to heavy rainfall to portions of the region. Over the next 24 to 36 hours a second strong low pressure system moved to the north and east bringing heavy rain, destructive winds, high surf, flooding, and thunderstorms to the Bay Area. Numerous reports were received of downed trees and power lines. Winds were recorded between 60 and 100 mph. Downed trees resulted in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 12 miles E of Gualala.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass pushed into Central California on the morning of New Year's Day. This resulted in a hard freeze taking place across the southern portion of the San Joaquin Valley where several locations had minimum temperatures below 28 Deg. F. In addition to the cold temperatures, locally dense fog impacted travel for a few hours as some residual low level moisture remained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold airmass that was in place over the San Joaquin Valley continued to dry out during the morning of January 2. With clear skies prevailing and light winds overnight, temperatures lowered into the 20's in many locations in the central and south San Joaquin Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped southeast off the California coast on January 11. As the low was off the central California coast it helped mix out the atmosphere over the San Joaquin Valley and allowed for the fog and low clouds which had persisted since the previous day in some areas to dissipate. This system also produced strong offshore pressure gradients which resulted in strong downslope winds near the Grapevine area in the Kern  County Mountains and in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. Several stations measured wind gusts between 35 and 50 mph during the afternoon and evening of January 11 and a pair of ridge top sites reported gusts near 60 mph. The winds diminished by late evening as the low moved inland across southern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grapevine Peak RAWS reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approached the central California coast during the early morning of January 14 then dropped southeast and moved into southern California during the afternoon. As the low dropped southeastward along the coast it spread deep moisture inland producing heavy snow in the Kern County Mountains above 4000 feet. Interstate 5 was closed for 7.5 hours as numerous accidents resulted in hundreds of stranded and overturned vehicles. In addition to the heavy snow, strong winds produced local whiteout conditions in the Kern County Mountains and gusts above 60 mph were reported in the Bear Valley Springs area, and several other locations in the Kern County Mountains and south end of the San Joaquin Valley measured wind gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bear Mountain reported a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large tree across both lanes of Old Stage Road near the intersection of Jack Ranch Road south of California Hot Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through central California during the evening of January 20 early morning of January 21 bringing a brief period of moderate to locally heavy precipitation along with strong wind gusts. Several reports of damaging winds were received during this time frame as winds downed several trees and power poles in the San Joaquin Valley where several stations measured gusts above 35 mph. There were also several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts and some low impact indicator sites measured gusts between 75 and 90 mph. Rainfall amounts varied in the San Joaquin Valley as some locations in the east central valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch while most of the west side of the valley was rain shadowed and did not pick up much rain. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between a half inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation while several locations above 7000 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of beneficial snowfall. The precipitation ended by the evening of January 21 as the frontal system moved out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bear Valley reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front swept through California on January 5th bringing widespread rainfall and gusty winds. Unstable air behind the frontal passage and sufficient low level shear allowed shallow thunderstorms to develop over the coastal waters, some of which contained rotating cells. Two waterspouts developed and made landfall as tornadoes on the 6th. Additionally, heavy rainfall and strong winds caused roadway flooding, minor debris flows, and numerous downed trees across the region. This storm system caused two fatalities; one caused by a downed tree in Berkeley and another man died in Santa Rosa Creek due to rising waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lane submurged in water I 280N and I 380E connector.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate to strong atmospheric river impacted much of California in the middle of the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approached the central California coast during the early morning of January 14 then dropped southeast and moved into southern California during the afternoon. As the low dropped southeastward along the coast it spread deep moisture inland producing heavy snow in the Kern County Mountains above 4000 feet. Interstate 5 was closed for 7.5 hours as numerous accidents resulted in hundreds of stranded and overturned vehicles. In addition to the heavy snow, strong winds produced local whiteout conditions in the Kern County Mountains and gusts above 60 mph were reported in the Bear Valley Springs area, and several other locations in the Kern County Mountains and south end of the San Joaquin Valley measured wind gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported several hundred vehicles stranded along Interstate 5 near 4000 feet near Lebec with numerous accidents and overturned vehicles in the area. Interstate 5 was closed for 7.5 hours in both directions. An estimated 2 to 4 inches of snow was reported by stranded motorists.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The California Highway Patrol station in Grapevine reported a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a deep moisture fetch pushed into central California during the afternoon of January 16 and brought moderate to heavy precipitation along with strong winds to much of the area through the afternoon of January 17. Several reports of roadway flooding were received during the morning of January 17 when the heaviest precipitation occurred. Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in the Ferguson Fire burn area in Mariposa County and State Route 140 was closed for over 11 hours. The precipitation turned to showers and isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of January 17. One thunderstorm produced a tornado east of Clovis which was rated as EF-1 following a storm survey of the damage it produced. Most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between half an inch to an inch of rain while the Southern Sierra Nevada had 2 to 4 inches of liquid precipitation and the Kern County Mountains had 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet where several locations in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada above 7000 feet picked up 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall. There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a power line downed on the roadway on Applegate Road near the intersection of Bailey Road south of Atwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge built inland into California on January 22. This ridge remained dominant over the region for several days and resulted in a period of dry weather. However, inversion conditions prevailed in the San Joaquin Valley and with mainly clear skies and light winds dense fog became prevalent in some areas during the nighttime and morning hours. During the morning of January 23, dense fog impacted travel near Hanford and Visalia and a 5 vehicle accident took place northeast of Hanford in dense fog. The fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several school districts delayed bus service. California Highway Patrol reported a 5 vehicle accident occurred at the intersection of 6th Ave. and Excelsior Ave. 7 miles NE of Hanford with visibility around 250 feet at the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a deep moisture fetch pushed into central California during the afternoon of January 16 and brought moderate to heavy precipitation along with strong winds to much of the area through the afternoon of January 17. Several reports of roadway flooding were received during the morning of January 17 when the heaviest precipitation occurred. Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in the Ferguson Fire burn area in Mariposa County and State Route 140 was closed for over 11 hours. The precipitation turned to showers and isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of January 17. One thunderstorm produced a tornado east of Clovis which was rated as EF-1 following a storm survey of the damage it produced. Most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between half an inch to an inch of rain while the Southern Sierra Nevada had 2 to 4 inches of liquid precipitation and the Kern County Mountains had 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet where several locations in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada above 7000 feet picked up 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall. There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS reported a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a deep moisture fetch pushed into central California during the afternoon of January 16 and brought moderate to heavy precipitation along with strong winds to much of the area through the afternoon of January 17. Several reports of roadway flooding were received during the morning of January 17 when the heaviest precipitation occurred. Flash flooding and debris flows were reported in the Ferguson Fire burn area in Mariposa County and State Route 140 was closed for over 11 hours. The precipitation turned to showers and isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of January 17. One thunderstorm produced a tornado east of Clovis which was rated as EF-1 following a storm survey of the damage it produced. Most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between half an inch to an inch of rain while the Southern Sierra Nevada had 2 to 4 inches of liquid precipitation and the Kern County Mountains had 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 7000 feet where several locations in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada above 7000 feet picked up 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall. There were also several reports of post-frontal wind gusts exceeding 50 mph in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts exceeding 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horse Meadow SNOTEL (8400 feet) picked up 12.0 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies allowed for dense fog to become more prevalent in the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 29. Fog reduced visibility below a eighth of a mile at several locations and several school districts either opened late or delayed or cancelled bus service. The dense fog also resulted in at least two major car accidents. Once again, the fog dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Visalia. Several school districts opened late or delayed or cancelled morning bus service due to dense fog. California Highway Patrol reported fog related two car accidents near Shafter and near Alpaugh with visibility as low as 100 feet.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front swept through California on January 5th bringing widespread rainfall and gusty winds. Unstable air behind the frontal passage and sufficient low level shear allowed shallow thunderstorms to develop over the coastal waters, some of which contained rotating cells. Two waterspouts developed and made landfall as tornadoes on the 6th. Additionally, heavy rainfall and strong winds caused roadway flooding, minor debris flows, and numerous downed trees across the region. This storm system caused two fatalities; one caused by a downed tree in Berkeley and another man died in Santa Rosa Creek due to rising waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in Sr 152 between Watsonville Rd and Santa Teresa. Road closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate to strong atmospheric river impacted much of California in the middle of the month. A weak surface low developed off the coast on January 15th bringing moderate to heavy rainfall to portions of the region. Over the next 24 to 36 hours a second strong low pressure system moved to the north and east bringing heavy rain, destructive winds, high surf, flooding, and thunderstorms to the Bay Area. Numerous reports were received of downed trees and power lines. Winds were recorded between 60 and 100 mph. Downed trees resulted in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the highway off ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep shortwave trough over the eastern Pacific moved on shore the 5th and then spread through the Sierra Nevada and northeastern California the 6th into the 7th. This system brought localized high winds and heavy snowfall to the Sierra Nevada and northeastern California through the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 14.5 and 18 inches fell near and west of California Highway 89 between the 6th and 7th, with 22 to 29 inches falling above 7000 feet. Below 7000 feet east of California Highway 89 and south of Interstate 80, between 7.5 and 26 inches of snow fell. South of US Highway 50, 4.5 inches of snow fell at Meyers and a foot of snow fell at Heavenly Ski Resort. Interstate 80 was closed from Colfax to the Nevada State line between 1345PST on the 6th and 0700PST on the 7th due to low visibility and heavy snow. California Highway 89 closed on the west shore of Lake Tahoe with California Highway 88 closed over Carson Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system pushed into northern California during the morning of January 5. This system pushed a precipitation band through central California during the afternoon and evening of January 5 which produced a quarter to half of an inch of rain across much of the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County Mountains and half an inch to an inch of liquid precipitation across the southern Sierra Nevada and an estimated 12 to 18 inches of snow fell above 7000 feet from SNOTEL observations. The Kern County Deserts were rain shadowed for much of this event and most areas only picked up a few hundredths of an inch of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree from runoff blocking the roadway on Old Highway at Mormon Bar crossing.

A weak surface low developed off the coast on January 15th bringing moderate to heavy rainfall to portions of the region. Over the next 24 to 36 hours a second strong low pressure system moved to the north and east bringing heavy rain, destructive winds, high surf, flooding, and thunderstorms to the Bay Area. Numerous reports were received of downed trees and power lines. Winds were recorded between 60 and 100 mph. Downed trees resulted in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding at SR 17 N and Summit Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with an associated cold front moved through the region from February 12th to the 15th bringing widespread flooding and debris flows. Multiple mainstem rivers flooded prompting evacuations from local officials. Strong wind gusts caused downed trees, power outages, and structural damage. Additionally, a tree fell on a car causing one fatality and one serious injury on Highway 17 while another downed tree caused a serious multi-car traffic accident that resulted in another fatality as well as major injuries. The areas peaks received upwards of 10 inches of rainfall and widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed. Mount Saint Helena recorded wind gusts of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding in lane 3, 4-5 inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved through the region near the end of the month with the bulk of the impacts felt in the North Bay. Widespread flooding was reported from minor roadway flooding to major flooding of mainstem rivers. The Russian River reached major flood stage flooding dozens of homes and businesses prompting mandatory evacuations and rescues. Venado received over 20 inches of rain in 48 hours. In addition to heavy rainfall, this system brought gusty winds with widespread gusts exceeding 50 mph and over 70 mph in the North Bay Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reports 2 of 3 lanes flooded along NB 101 on the Shiloh Rd off ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed late Tuesday afternoon on Feb 17th along a north-south oriented convergence boundary. Thunderstorms produced accumulating hail and lightning over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating hail reported on the 680 corridor in Contra Costa.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported in Carmel Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the low pressure system that brought significant rainfall to the San Joaquin Valley and wintry conditions to the higher elevations, a strong cold front pushed through the area on February 10. While this system did provide for more rainfall and higher elevations snow as many area in the San Joaquin Valley picked up between another quarter to half inch of rainfall. The main impact from this system was strong winds with the cold front as it pushed through the area during the afternoon of February 10. Several reporting stations in the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills  measured gusts above 35 mph and several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured gusts above 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with an associated cold front moved through the region from February 12th to the 15th bringing widespread flooding and debris flows. Multiple mainstem rivers flooded prompting evacuations from local officials. Strong wind gusts caused downed trees, power outages, and structural damage. Additionally, a tree fell on a car causing one fatality and one serious injury on Highway 17 while another downed tree caused a serious multi-car traffic accident that resulted in another fatality as well as major injuries. The areas peaks received upwards of 10 inches of rainfall and widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed. Mount Saint Helena recorded wind gusts of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on on ramp of I880 S and 7th St, 3 to 4 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms in February brought heavy snow to unusually low elevations, making a great impact in some areas. This was the second of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at McCloud reported 24.3 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 13/0800 PST.  The cooperative observer at Dunsmuir reported 11.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 13/0800 PST. A member of the public in Dunsmuir reported 18.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 13/0749 PST with tree branches down everywhere. A spotter 2SSE Mount Shasta City reported 24.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 13/0717 PST.  A spotter in Mount Shasta City reported 12.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 13/0400 PST.  The Mount Shasta Ski Resort at elevation 5500 feet reported 36.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 13/0700 PST, closing down the resort. A spotter 3NW Mount Shasta City reported 14.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 13/0000 PST.  Interstate 5 was closed due to heavy snow for about 12 hours from the night of the 12th until mid-morning on the 13th.  Over 1000 customers lost power around Mount Shasta City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with an associated cold front moved through the region from February 12th to the 15th bringing widespread flooding and debris flows. Multiple mainstem rivers flooded prompting evacuations from local officials. Strong wind gusts caused downed trees, power outages, and structural damage. Additionally, a tree fell on a car causing one fatality and one serious injury on Highway 17 while another downed tree caused a serious multi-car traffic accident that resulted in another fatality as well as major injuries. The areas peaks received upwards of 10 inches of rainfall and widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed. Mount Saint Helena recorded wind gusts of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near creek roadway flooded, D St at San Antonio Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved through the region near the end of the month with the bulk of the impacts felt in the North Bay. Widespread flooding was reported from minor roadway flooding to major flooding of mainstem rivers. The Russian River reached major flood stage flooding dozens of homes and businesses prompting mandatory evacuations and rescues. Venado received over 20 inches of rain in 48 hours. In addition to heavy rainfall, this system brought gusty winds with widespread gusts exceeding 50 mph and over 70 mph in the North Bay Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding at US-101 S and Petaluma Blvd off ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact mini atmospheric river impacted Southern California January 14th and 15th which brought heavy rain and snow to the region. Seal Beach reported 2 inches of rain in 2 hours, which caused extensive flash flooding. Water was up to doorways outside of homes, and the Pacific Coast Highway was closed for over a day in Huntington Beach due to flooding. Rainfall rates remained below flash flood thresholds at the Holy and Cranston burn scars during this mini-AR event. Dense fog was reported in the Cajon Pass on the morning of January 16th, which resulted in a 19-car pileup and 35 patients with minor to moderate injuries. ||A larger atmospheric river moved into Southern California on January 17th, bringing heavy rain and snow to the region. Extensive flash flooding occurred due to the heavy rain. Swift water rescues occurred on the Santa Ana River in Riverside due to flooding. Rainfall rates exceeded flash flooding thresholds for the Holy fire burn scar on the 17th.||Overall, 6 to 12 inches of rain fell in the Santa Ana and San Bernardino County Mountains between January 14-17. Snowfall amounts in the mountains ranged from 4 to 12 inches above 6500 ft. Major surf and coastal flooding occurred during this event as well, which resulted in extensive damage of the Ocean Beach Pier in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burns Canyon Mesonet reported a peak wind gust of 65 knots between 1750 and 1850 PST on January 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled over the area during the early morning of February 11 behind a cold front which pushed through the region on the previous day. Clearing skies and decreased winds allowed for temperatures in the San Joaquin Valley to lower to below the freezing mark during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the area brought an extended period of heavy snow to northern California. It was also very windy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Shasta Ski Resort at elevation 5500 feet reported 36 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 26/0700 PST, another 30 inches in 24 hours ending at 27/0700 PST,  then another 45 inches in 24 hours ending at 28/0700 PST, for a storm total of 111 inches of snow over 72 hours.  A spotter ESE Mount Shasta City reported 4.0 inches of snow in 3 hours ending at 25/1900 PST. The snow then turned to rain.  A member of the public 3SSW Weed reported 16.8 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 26/0700 PST, then another 14.6 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 27/0700 PST. A member of the public 5W Dunsmuir reported 10.8 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 25/1400 PST.  A member of the public 5NW McCloud reported 36.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 26/1346 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the area brought an extended period of heavy snow to northern California. It was also very windy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong winds recorded here occurred during a winter storm with snow and blowing snow. The Timber Mountain RAWS recorded a gust to 68 mph at 25/1203 PST and a gust to 62 mph at 25/1303 PST. A gust to 61 mph was recorded at 27/1403 PST, a gust to 64 mph was recorded at 27/1503 PST, and a gust to 62 mph was recorded at 27/1603 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with an associated cold front moved through the region from February 12th to the 15th bringing widespread flooding and debris flows. Multiple mainstem rivers flooded prompting evacuations from local officials. Strong wind gusts caused downed trees, power outages, and structural damage. Additionally, a tree fell on a car causing one fatality and one serious injury on Highway 17 while another downed tree caused a serious multi-car traffic accident that resulted in another fatality as well as major injuries. The areas peaks received upwards of 10 inches of rainfall and widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed. Mount Saint Helena recorded wind gusts of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EB Tesla at Vasco Rd, all lanes flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved through the region near the end of the month with the bulk of the impacts felt in the North Bay. Widespread flooding was reported from minor roadway flooding to major flooding of mainstem rivers. The Russian River reached major flood stage flooding dozens of homes and businesses prompting mandatory evacuations and rescues. Venado received over 20 inches of rain in 48 hours. In addition to heavy rainfall, this system brought gusty winds with widespread gusts exceeding 50 mph and over 70 mph in the North Bay Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding at Shoreline Hwy intersection with Bay Rd, vehicle stalled in 2 feet of water in roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact mini atmospheric river impacted Southern California January 14th and 15th which brought heavy rain and snow to the region. Seal Beach reported 2 inches of rain in 2 hours, which caused extensive flash flooding. Water was up to doorways outside of homes, and the Pacific Coast Highway was closed for over a day in Huntington Beach due to flooding. Rainfall rates remained below flash flood thresholds at the Holy and Cranston burn scars during this mini-AR event. Dense fog was reported in the Cajon Pass on the morning of January 16th, which resulted in a 19-car pileup and 35 patients with minor to moderate injuries. ||A larger atmospheric river moved into Southern California on January 17th, bringing heavy rain and snow to the region. Extensive flash flooding occurred due to the heavy rain. Swift water rescues occurred on the Santa Ana River in Riverside due to flooding. Rainfall rates exceeded flash flooding thresholds for the Holy fire burn scar on the 17th.||Overall, 6 to 12 inches of rain fell in the Santa Ana and San Bernardino County Mountains between January 14-17. Snowfall amounts in the mountains ranged from 4 to 12 inches above 6500 ft. Major surf and coastal flooding occurred during this event as well, which resulted in extensive damage of the Ocean Beach Pier in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harrison Park mesonet station reported a peak wind gust of 55 knots between 1800 and 1900 PST on January 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern California and brought widespread rain, a few inches of snow, and frost to the region. Total rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches, locally 4 inches in the San Diego County Mountains. Hourly rainfall thresholds for the Holy burn scar were exceeded in a couple of canyons, with the highest threshold of 0.71 per hour in Horsethief Canyon. Widespread frost occurred on February 7th, and reached the coastal areas of San Diego and Orange Counties. Icy roads on I-8 resulted in traffic accidents and two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Horsethief Canyon recorded a peak hourly rainfall rate of 0.71 per hour between 1340 and 1440 PST on February 4th. This exceeded established flash flooding hourly rainfall thresholds of 0.50 per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern California and brought widespread rain, a few inches of snow, and frost to the region. Total rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches, locally 4 inches in the San Diego County Mountains. Hourly rainfall thresholds for the Holy burn scar were exceeded in a couple of canyons, with the highest threshold of 0.71 per hour in Horsethief Canyon. Widespread frost occurred on February 7th, and reached the coastal areas of San Diego and Orange Counties. Icy roads on I-8 resulted in traffic accidents and two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water overtopped at Trabuco Canyon Road according to Orange County Fire Authority. Trabuco Canyon gauge reported 0.90 inches in 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major atmospheric river impacted Southern California February 13th through 15th bringing heavy rain and high winds. Over the course of 48 hours, many mountain locales recorded 8 to 10 inches of rain, with Palomar Mountain (San Diego County) recording over 10 inches of rain in 24 hours. Palm Springs recorded 3.7 inches of rain in 24 hours. This heavy rain resulted in major flash flooding and damage to roads. High snow levels resulted in flooding in the San Bernardino Mountains. Flash flood thresholds were exceeded in the Holy Fire burn scar, with an hourly maximum rainfall rate of 1.02 inches at Leach Canyon. Major flash flooding occurred at the Holy Fire burn scar. Roads were also damaged from this storm, with many roads closed for weeks for major repairs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches occurred on the desert slope of Mt San Jacinto and the Coachella Valley floor. This resulted in massive flooding and water flow into the Whitewater river (up to 10,000 CFS). Flooding severely damaged highway 111 causing a week long closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and winds diminished across the area during the evening of February 17 as skies cleared out. This resulted in the freezing of leftover rain and melted snow on several roads  which resulted in several roads in the Kern County Mountains being closed for several hours overnight and into the morning hours of February 18. Black ice also resulted in a fatal two vehicle accident near Oakhurst on State Route 49 during the morning of February 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 5 was closed by California Highway Patrol between Grapevine and Castaic for several hours between the early evening of February 17 to the late morning of February 18 due to refreezing of rain and wet snow which led to the formation of black ice on several roads in the Kern County Mountains. Several vehicles spun out or crashed due to the black ice on Interstate 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass settled in over the area on February 18 as a northerly flow prevailed behind the trough that had brought several days of unsettled weather across central California. Clearing skies and decreased winds during the early morning hours of February 19 resulted in sub-freezing minimum temperatures across much of the San Joaquin Valley that morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped southward through the Great Basin on February 20. This system resulted in a brief periods of strong winds over the crest of the Southern Sierra Nevada and below the passes in Kern County. Several locations in Kern County measured peak winds gusts above 40 mph while a few low impact indicator sites reported gusts exceeding 60 mph. This was followed by a very cold system which pushed southward through central California on February 21. While liquid precipitation with this system was not very significant, it did produce snow at elevations as low as 1000 feet and several locations in the southern Sierra Foothills and Kern County Mountains picked up between 2 and 6 inches of new snowfall between the afternoon of February 20 and the afternoon of February 21. This system moved east of the area during the evening of February 21 with a cold airmass being left behind in it's wake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Peak RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with an associated cold front moved through the region from February 12th to the 15th bringing widespread flooding and debris flows. Multiple mainstem rivers flooded prompting evacuations from local officials. Strong wind gusts caused downed trees, power outages, and structural damage. Additionally, a tree fell on a car causing one fatality and one serious injury on Highway 17 while another downed tree caused a serious multi-car traffic accident that resulted in another fatality as well as major injuries. The areas peaks received upwards of 10 inches of rainfall and widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed. Mount Saint Helena recorded wind gusts of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding on Alemany at 101 and 280 entrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major atmospheric river impacted Southern California February 13th through 15th bringing heavy rain and high winds. Over the course of 48 hours, many mountain locales recorded 8 to 10 inches of rain, with Palomar Mountain (San Diego County) recording over 10 inches of rain in 24 hours. Palm Springs recorded 3.7 inches of rain in 24 hours. This heavy rain resulted in major flash flooding and damage to roads. High snow levels resulted in flooding in the San Bernardino Mountains. Flash flood thresholds were exceeded in the Holy Fire burn scar, with an hourly maximum rainfall rate of 1.02 inches at Leach Canyon. Major flash flooding occurred at the Holy Fire burn scar. Roads were also damaged from this storm, with many roads closed for weeks for major repairs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 6 inches occurred in 12 hours and up to 1 inch per hour rates. This was the most significant flash flooding and debris flows since the December 6, 2018 event. The Holy fire scar flooded and sent debris flows through Temescal Canyon road and into homes and over roadways. Riverside County reported up to 70 million dollars in flood control structure damage in the 8 canyons affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold and dry airmass which built into the area on February 5 and brought sub-freezing temperatures to much of the San Joaquin Valley on February 6 remained over the area with little moderation through the morning of February 7. With clear skies and light winds prevailing, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark again for a few hours during the morning in the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees.

Additionally, a tree fell on a car causing one fatality and one serious injury on Highway 17 while another downed tree caused a serious multi-car traffic accident that resulted in another fatality as well as major injuries. The areas peaks received upwards of 10 inches of rainfall and widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed. Mount Saint Helena recorded wind gusts of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reports a severe traffic alert and flooding on CA-12 from Watmaugh Rd to CA-121 at Millerick Rd, all lanes closed in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved through the region near the end of the month with the bulk of the impacts felt in the North Bay. Widespread flooding was reported from minor roadway flooding to major flooding of mainstem rivers. The Russian River reached major flood stage flooding dozens of homes and businesses prompting mandatory evacuations and rescues. Venado received over 20 inches of rain in 48 hours. In addition to heavy rainfall, this system brought gusty winds with widespread gusts exceeding 50 mph and over 70 mph in the North Bay Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Russian River at Guerneville hit flood stage (32ft) at approximately 1600 hrs on February 26th, 2019. The gauge crested at 45.38 feet at 22:30 pm February 27th, 2019. Local law enforcement indicated the town of Guerneville became an island and was only accessible by boat or helicopter. The media reported approximately 4,000 structures were impacted by flood waters. The gauge did not fall below flood stage until after 1:30 pm on March 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low moved into Southern California on February 21st and brought heavy snow, rain and hail. Top snowfall totals were above 8000 feet at locations such as Green Valley Lake, Barton Flats, and Running Springs. These locations received 18-23 inches of snow. Wrightwood received less snow, around 3 to 7 inches. Big Bear Lake received 12-18 inches and Julian received 8-12 inches. An unusual 3 to 7 inches fell in Lucerne Valley. Thundersnow occurred in the San Bernardino Mountains. Snow levels went down to 1000 feet in the Orange County foothills, and snow levels went down to 2000 ft in the San Diego County Mountains. Reports of small hail in the coastal and valley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 12 to 24 inches impacted the San Bernardino mountains. Highway 18 and 38 were closed February 21 to 23 leaving Big Bear city shut off from traffic (highway 18 was already closed west of Big Bear City). Several small avalanches and rockslides occurred on Highway 38 causing excessive snow removal. Motorists and those recreating in Big Bear did not have an exit route until Friday afternoon after 3 pm when highway 18 north of Big Bear city re-opened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major atmospheric river impacted Southern California February 13th through 15th bringing heavy rain and high winds. Over the course of 48 hours, many mountain locales recorded 8 to 10 inches of rain, with Palomar Mountain (San Diego County) recording over 10 inches of rain in 24 hours. Palm Springs recorded 3.7 inches of rain in 24 hours. This heavy rain resulted in major flash flooding and damage to roads. High snow levels resulted in flooding in the San Bernardino Mountains. Flash flood thresholds were exceeded in the Holy Fire burn scar, with an hourly maximum rainfall rate of 1.02 inches at Leach Canyon. Major flash flooding occurred at the Holy Fire burn scar. Roads were also damaged from this storm, with many roads closed for weeks for major repairs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust at Wrightwood ASOS was 53 knots between 1200 and 1300 PST on February 14th. Extensive wind damage was reported at Mountain High Resort in Wrightwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold system brought snow to most of extreme northern California.  It was heavy at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mt. Shasta Ski Park reported 10.0 inches of snow in 24 hours, ending at 04/0600 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed in the Antelope Valley in Los Angeles County during the evening of January 31 and pushed northward into the Kern County Deserts by early morning on February 1. The fog impacted travel along State Route 58 north of Edwards AFB and in the  Rosamond area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Edwards Air Force Base reported visibility below a quarter mile at both the north and south runways. California Highway Patrol reported visibility down to 200 feet along State Route 58 north of the base and down to 500 feet in the Rosamond area. The fog dissipated by late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and dry air pushed into the San Joaquin Valley behind a strong low pressure center which moved through the area on February 4 and 5. Skies cleared out and winds diminished during the morning of February 6 allowing for temperatures to plunge below the freezing mark for a few hours in the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm system moved through central California between the afternoon of February 8 and the evening of February 9. This system brought between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall to much of the San Joaquin Valley. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation and snow levels lowered to near 3000 feet by the time the precipitation tapered off on the evening of February 9. A few locations in the Southern Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon picked up over a foot of new snow while many locations in the Sierra foothills and the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between 2 and 6 inches of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yosemite Valley measured 14 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer westerly flow aided in a persistent influx of moisture across the steep coastal terrain of Northwest California. Heavy rain occurred as a result, leading to rapid rises on creeks, streams and rivers. Numerous instances of flooding were observed, which resulted in road closures and one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 36 closed due to rock slide 3 miles west of Forest Glen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the mountains and extended down into lower elevations, causing widespread travel problems and road closures. Thunderstorms brought heavy rain and small hail with widespread road flooding. Gusty winds brought down trees and caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and whiteout conditions caused I80 to be closed for February 15th for the morning through 6 pm. Avalanche control and a fallen tree also kept closed Highway 50 much of the day. These closures caused major impacts to travel and interstate commerce.||Castle Peak had a storm total of 45 inches of new snow. Squaw Valley Ski Resort at 8,000 feet reported 30 inches of new snow over 24 hours. At 6200 feet 20 inches was reported over 24 hours. Soda Springs had a storm total of 40 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid/upper low with a very cold airmass moved through in early February bringing snow to lower elevation peaks across the region prompting a rare Winter Weather Advisory. Junipero Serra Peak received around a foot of snow. Rainfall just ahead of this system also brought roadway flooding and minor debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slushy snow 3 miles SW of San Francisco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low moved into Southern California on February 21st and brought heavy snow, rain and hail. Top snowfall totals were above 8000 feet at locations such as Green Valley Lake, Barton Flats, and Running Springs. These locations received 18-23 inches of snow. Wrightwood received less snow, around 3 to 7 inches. Big Bear Lake received 12-18 inches and Julian received 8-12 inches. An unusual 3 to 7 inches fell in Lucerne Valley. Thundersnow occurred in the San Bernardino Mountains. Snow levels went down to 1000 feet in the Orange County foothills, and snow levels went down to 2000 ft in the San Diego County Mountains. Reports of small hail in the coastal and valley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intestate 8 was closed for 12 hours due to snow covered and icy roads. Numerous accidents including at Willows road at 2600 fee where video capture a school bus sliding into the hillside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm system moved through central California between the afternoon of February 8 and the evening of February 9. This system brought between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall to much of the San Joaquin Valley. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation and snow levels lowered to near 3000 feet by the time the precipitation tapered off on the evening of February 9. A few locations in the Southern Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon picked up over a foot of new snow while many locations in the Sierra foothills and the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between 2 and 6 inches of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tuolumne Meadows SNOTEL (8600 feet) picked up an estimated 14 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with an associated cold front moved through the region from February 12th to the 15th bringing widespread flooding and debris flows. Multiple mainstem rivers flooded prompting evacuations from local officials. Strong wind gusts caused downed trees, power outages, and structural damage. Additionally, a tree fell on a car causing one fatality and one serious injury on Highway 17 while another downed tree caused a serious multi-car traffic accident that resulted in another fatality as well as major injuries. The areas peaks received upwards of 10 inches of rainfall and widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed. Mount Saint Helena recorded wind gusts of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lot of standing water on the SB I-280 off ramp at Saratoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major atmospheric river impacted Southern California February 13th through 15th bringing heavy rain and high winds. Over the course of 48 hours, many mountain locales recorded 8 to 10 inches of rain, with Palomar Mountain (San Diego County) recording over 10 inches of rain in 24 hours. Palm Springs recorded 3.7 inches of rain in 24 hours. This heavy rain resulted in major flash flooding and damage to roads. High snow levels resulted in flooding in the San Bernardino Mountains. Flash flood thresholds were exceeded in the Holy Fire burn scar, with an hourly maximum rainfall rate of 1.02 inches at Leach Canyon. Major flash flooding occurred at the Holy Fire burn scar. Roads were also damaged from this storm, with many roads closed for weeks for major repairs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm channels were inundated by flash flooding with up to 2 inches of rain in 3 hours. Streets were closed and homes threatened by waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the low pressure system that brought significant rainfall to the San Joaquin Valley and wintry conditions to the higher elevations, a strong cold front pushed through the area on February 10. While this system did provide for more rainfall and higher elevations snow as many area in the San Joaquin Valley picked up between another quarter to half inch of rainfall. The main impact from this system was strong winds with the cold front as it pushed through the area during the afternoon of February 10. Several reporting stations in the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills  measured gusts above 35 mph and several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured gusts above 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported rocks and mud on the westbound lane of State Route 198 and some debris on the eastbound lane around 10 miles WSW of Coalinga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved through Southern California March 20th and 21st and brought heavy rain, thunderstorms, and mountain snow to the area. The heaviest rainfall and snowfall occurred on the 20th, with just light showers on the 21st. Rainfall totals of 0.25 to 0.50 inch fell in the coastal areas (particularly San Diego County coastline), and up to an inch of rain fell in the valley and mountain areas. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1-4 inches above 5500 feet. Small hail and lightning occurred in thunderstorms on the 20th. Urban street flooding occurred in Orange County and at the Holy Fire Burn Scar. Rainfall rates of 0.70/hr occurred at the burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CalTrans reported accidents on Highway 241 northbound due to hail on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms brought flooding in Shasta County. Over a foot of snow caused travel problems at the Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 1.55 inches of rain measured in 45 minutes near downtown Redding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms brought flooding in Shasta County. Over a foot of snow caused travel problems at the Sierra passes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and very moist upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on March 5. This system pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of sub-tropical origin which pushed into the area during the evening of March 5 then stalled over the southern portion of central California during the morning of March 6 as a strong southwest upper jet was overhead. This resulted in widespread heavy precipitation over Kern County with moderate to locally heavy precipitation further north over an already saturated ground producing widespread flooding, debris flows, rock slides and mud slides across the area. Several roads were closed as a result of the flooding. Thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon producing additional moderate to locally heavy rainfall. Much of the Southern Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with the snow level running between 8000 and 8500 feet for much of the event. Between 1 to 3 inches of rain fell in the adjacent foothills and across the Kern County Mountains. Kelso Creek to the southeast of Lake Isabella filled up that a potential levee breach led to an evacuation of the communities of Weldon Valley Ranchos, Valley Estates and Power Tracts within the town of Kelso Creek. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain while locally heavier amounts fell in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley and across the Indian Wells Valley, and as a result road flooding was widespread in Kern County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system dropped southward through the Great Basin region between the afternoon of March 12 and the morning of March 13. This system interacted with a strengthening offshore high pressure center to produce a strong surface pressure gradient over the Kern County Mountains and Deserts which resulted in a period of very strong winds over the area with several reports of wind gusts exceeding 70 mph along ridge tops and below passes. There were also reports of several big rigs being blown over or flipped over by the strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cache Creek CHP station reported a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high impact mini atmospheric river impacted Southern California January 14th and 15th which brought heavy rain and snow to the region. Seal Beach reported 2 inches of rain in 2 hours, which caused extensive flash flooding. Water was up to doorways outside of homes, and the Pacific Coast Highway was closed for over a day in Huntington Beach due to flooding. Rainfall rates remained below flash flood thresholds at the Holy and Cranston burn scars during this mini-AR event. Dense fog was reported in the Cajon Pass on the morning of January 16th, which resulted in a 19-car pileup and 35 patients with minor to moderate injuries. ||A larger atmospheric river moved into Southern California on January 17th, bringing heavy rain and snow to the region. Extensive flash flooding occurred due to the heavy rain. Swift water rescues occurred on the Santa Ana River in Riverside due to flooding. Rainfall rates exceeded flash flooding thresholds for the Holy fire burn scar on the 17th.||Overall, 6 to 12 inches of rain fell in the Santa Ana and San Bernardino County Mountains between January 14-17. Snowfall amounts in the mountains ranged from 4 to 12 inches above 6500 ft. Major surf and coastal flooding occurred during this event as well, which resulted in extensive damage of the Ocean Beach Pier in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Peak mesonet reported a peak wind gust of 54 knots between 1730 and 1830 PST on January 17th.
