EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moisture plume moved through central California on February 13 and 14 as a strong low pressure system approached the area. Heavy rainfall and strong southerly winds impacted the area as this moisture moved through the area. Several reports of winds gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported and snow levels rose to near 9000 feet which resulted in widespread flooding in the area and several road closures from flooding and debris flows were reported. A strong cold front pushed through the area during the morning of February 15 which resulted in snow levels plummeting to around 5000 feet. Thunderstorms broke out in the colder and unstable post-frontal airmass and several reports of small hail were observed in the San Joaquin Valley. In addition, a small EF 0 tornado briefly touched down n ear Yosemite Lakes and funnel clouds were observed in the San Joaquin Valley. A colder system moved into the area on February 16 and snow levels lowered further down to 1500 feet. Low elevation snowfall on February 16 and 17 resulted in numerous road closures and travel delays and there were several reports of 10 to 20 inches of new snowfall reported in the Southern Sierra Nevada between the evening of February 15 and the evening of February 17. Rainfall totals between February 13 and February 17 were generally between 3 and 7 inches of liquid precipitation in the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada with 2 to 5 inches in the adjacent foothills. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a half inch and an inch and a half of rainfall during this period while the Tehachapi Mountains generally picked up between 1 to 2 inches of liquid precipitation during the 5 day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The APRS station in Taft reported a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system approached the central California coast during the morning of February 2. Ahead of the low, strong southerly winds impacted the Grapevine area along Interstate 5 for much of the morning. By late morning the strong winds spread northward into the Bakersfield area where there were numerous reports of downed trees and wind damage. As the main low moved inland during the day, moderate to heavy precipitation spread into area and produced several instances of roadway and nuisance flooding. Scattered thunderstorms brought additional rainfall and small hail to the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills during the late afternoon. One thunderstorm produced a brief small tornado south of Mariposa. Several stations in the San Joaquin Valley picked up over an inch of rain while several stations in the mountains measured  between 2 and 5 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell mainly as snow above 5000 feet where heavy snowfall was reported at several stations and several feet of new snowfall was estimated at several SNOTELS from this storm and the following storm which moved through the area on February 4 and 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crane Flat RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold Pacific Storm system dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 3 and slowly moved across the regions on February 4 and 5. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to portions of the San Joaquin Valley on February 4 and heavy snow to elevations as low as 2000 feet in the southern Sierra Nevada foothills on February 4 and 5. Between 4 and 6 feet of new snow fell above 5000 feet in the Southern Sierra Nevada.  The heavy wet snow also resulted in numerous power outages affecting close to 20300 customers in the Mariposa and Yosemite Park areas. Additionally, all roads leading into Yosemite National Park (including State Routes 120, 140 and 41) were closed due to heavy snowfall. Several other roads had chain requirements above 2000 feet. Several school districts in the southern Sierra foothills closed for the day on February 5 due to roads being close to impassible as a result of heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fish Camp (4980 feet) measured 48.0 inches of new snowfall between the morning of February 4 and the morning of February 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme fire weather conditions were forecast for Oct 9 and 10 as northern California was caught between a ridge over the eastern Pacific and a trough over the Great Basin. This caused the pressure gradients to tighten over the county warning area, which lead to strong northerly and easterly winds. With these winds forecast, dry conditions in place, and fuels being primed a Red Flag Warning was issued for most of interior northern California. Fortunately no major wildfires resulted from this but a controlled burn got out of control and utility power lines did suffer from some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station, Sac NWR, near Delevan reported a non-thunderstorm related wind gust up to 77 mph. Within Glenn County, a utility company reported tree and wind related damage to power lines near Artois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Critical fire weather conditions were forecast for Oct 23-24 and again on October 27-28 with the eastern Pacific High allowing trough to slide into the Great Basin/Nevada areas. This caused the pressure gradients to tighten over the county warning area, which lead to additional rounds of strong northerly and easterly winds. With these winds forecast, dry conditions still in place, and fuels still being primed a Red Flag Warning was issued for most of interior northern California. Fortunately no major wildfires resulted from this but utility power lines did suffer from wind and tree damage across much of northern California. Time stamps for events are based on strongest wind gusts that occurred between Oct 23-38.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Redding Airport a non-thunderstorm related wind gust up to 66 mph. Within Shasta and Tehama counties, a utility company reported tree and wind related damage to power lines near the Cottonwood, Deschutes, Jessup, Volta, Anderson, Girvan, Oregon Trail, Pit No 5, Stillwater, Gerber, Rawson, Red Bluff, and Volta-South areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure prevailed over California between June 10 and June 12 which resulted in much warmer than normal temperatures across the area during the period. High temperatures generally ranged between 102 and 107 degrees each day across much of the San Joaquin Valley prompting the first Excessive Heat Warning of the season. As a result of the heat, emergency cooling centers were opened each afternoon in several municipalities in the San Joaquin Valley during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 102 and 107 degrees at most locations between June 10 and June 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Because of continued increased releases at Pine Flat Dam. Runoff continued to produce  flooding at Lindy's Landing campground near Reedley, and the Kings River Golf and Country Club near Kingsburg well into June. The releases were cut back during the middle of the month and the flood waters receded by the evening of June 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresno and Tulare County Emergency Managers reported flooding along the Kings River downstream from Pine Flat Dam as a result of increased releases prompted by increased runoff from snow melt and recent rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system remained nearly stationary over southern California and continued to spread wrap around moisture into the area. This resulted in another afternoon of showers and thunderstorms on June 2. One cell produced a tornado east of Bakersfield while several others produced heavy rainfall and small hail. A slow moving thunderstorm produced flash flooding in downtown Coalinga which damaged several structures. The low moved east on June 3 and 4 which resulted in less moisture spreading  into the area and fewer thunderstorms on the following afternoons.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coalinga Fire Department reported all major streets in downtown Coalinga were completely flooded with several structures damaged by water after a thunderstorm dropped 1.36 inches of rainfall in one hour and 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of much below normal temperatures and frequent rainfall during the second half of May as an unseasonably cool airmass prevailed over the area, temperatures rose to above seasonal levels by early June. High pressure strengthened over the area on June 5 resulting in the first day of widespread triple digit heat prompting the first Heat Advisory of the season for much of the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 100 and 104 degrees at most locations on June 5.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high pressure and strong offshore flow resulted in an early season heat wave across the Bay Area from June 9th to the 11th. Multiple daily records were broken across the region and multiple power outages were reported due to the heat. Approximately 26,000 people across the region lost power on Monday followed by an additional 30,000 on Tuesday. Three fatalities were reported during the heat event. One man died as a direct result of heat related illness while two others drowned while attempting to cool down during the heat wave. Hot temperatures and dry grass resulted in a vegetation fire that spread to a residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system dropped slowly southward over central California on May 31. While widespread showers and thunderstorms developed that afternoon over the Sierra Nevada range and adjacent foothills as well as the west side hills of the San Joaquin Valley, no significant flooding took place and thunderstorms remained below severe limits. However, as the low moved south into southern California on June 1 and spread wrap around moisture into the area, slow moving thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours. One slow moving cell produced flash flooding in the Mojave area and another thunderstorm produced flash flooding near Kettlemen City in western Kings County. Both episodes resulted in some brief road closures. Several reports of hail between a quarter and a half inch in diameter and rainfall reports of up to half an inch of rain were observed with the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported water and debris across the roadway near Mojave West Park. A nearby weather stations measured 0.52 inches of rain in 20 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the Eastern Sierra Avalanche Center, a full course slab avalanche was accidentally triggered on a 40 degree slope by two snow travelers who were utilizing splitboards to travel in the Ripper Chute on the Dana Plateau at an elevation of 11600 feet MSL (above treeline on the northeast aspect of Mount Dana) at approximately 23 March 1030PST. One snow traveler was able to self-arrest, while the climber 10 feet below was swept the entire course of the couloir. The lower climber sustained minor injuries and was able to walk/glissade on a shovel out to the trailhead. Technical details of the avalanche were as follows. The weak layer was storm snow. The bed surface was old snow. The crown height of the avalanche was 2 feet. The avalanche average length and width were 1000 feet and 20 feet, respectively. The avalanche was estimated to be a D2 in destructive size (could bury, injure, or kill a person) and an R3 in relative size (medium, relative to the path). According to the report, there was precipitation in the form of snow with less than a 1 in per hour accumulation rate. Overcast skies, bad visibility, and blowing snow with strong southeast winds were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Great Basin brought high winds and single digit relative humidity to southern California. Gusty Santa Ana winds were reported in the mountains and passes, with peak gusts topping out 60-70 mph. The windiest location was Fremont Canyon at 73 mph. A few small wildfires broke out across the region. The Water Fire was the smallest, but most destructive fire during this time period, destroying 3 structures and 2 vehicles. The 46 Fire charred 300 acres and prompted the evacuations of approximately 3600 residents, with no structures destroyed or injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highland Springs recorded a peak wind gust of 63 knots around 0800 PST on 10/30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge over the Great Basin brought offshore flow and hot temperatures to Southern California June 9th through 12th. The Coachella Valley and San Diego County deserts were the warmest locations with highs 110-117. In the Inland Empire, highs ranged from 105-110. A few locations in the San Diego inland areas (Ramona, Escondido, Alpine) reached the mid to upper 90s during the 4-day heatwave. Many locations broke daily high temperature records; most notably, El Cajon broke its daily record on June 10th by 10 degrees. In San Diego County, a total of 36 people were treated for heat-related illnesses between June 9th and 12th.||Dense fog was observed at the coast and on higher coastal terrain on June 12th with very low visibilities (less than 1/8 of a mile at times). Fog lifted and visibility improved by 9 AM on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inland Empire locations recorded temperatures ranging from 100 to 110 during this 4-day heatwave. June 9th, Riverside reached 104 which was a record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms developed around Joshua Tree National Park during the afternoon hours on July 25th, and some of the stronger storms produced locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain was sufficient to produce an episode of flash flooding across the northern portion of the park. At 1530PST Joshua Tree Dispatch reported flash flooding on Park Boulevard (Route 12) near Mile Marker 4. No reports of accidents or injuries were received. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued earlier in the afternoon but the warning was for an area slightly to the east of where the flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms developed in the area around Joshua Tree National Park during the afternoon hours on July 25th. Some of the stronger storms produced locally heavy rains with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour; the heavy rain was sufficient to cause an episode of flash flooding in the park. According to the Joshua Tree Dispatch, at 1530PST flash flooding was observed on Park Boulevard (Route 12) near Mile Marker 4. No accidents or injuries were reported. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued earlier in the afternoon, but the warning actually covered an area that was slightly to the east of where the flooding was reported.

A multi-agency search and rescue was started that afternoon, but the victim was not recovered until the following day. The Sheriff's office noted that the water temperature was about 40 degrees Fahrenheit for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough passing over the Pacific Northwest brought breezy winds to the San Joaquin Valley. These conditions, combined with dry fuels led to a increased fire danger for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock Fire started Tuesday night June 25th, 4 miles Del Puerto Canyon Rd, which is west of Patterson. It grew to 2422 acres over 24 hours and according to a CalFire via a media interview, the fire was burning near several Native American archaeological sites belonging to the Yokuts. Crews took extra precautions not to disturb the soil of these sites and at the time of the media report, it was unknown if the fire had done any damage. Additionally, it was also noted in a media interview that some acres of ranch land were burned but no buildings or structures were.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers remained unusually cold and fast moving due to the deep Sierra snow pack continued melt, which contributed to multiple drownings over a stretch of days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On June 19th, a group of friends had been swimming in the Cosumnes River at Highway 49, near the Amador-El Dorado County line. Around 1130PST, a call was made to local authorities that a teenage boy was pulled under by rapids and disappeared. Two hours later, rescue teams were able to recover the victim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave troughs rotated through a main upper level trough brought showers and thunderstorms to the region the afternoon and evening of the 16th. Several storms lingered in place over areas due to weak steering flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that approximately 20 cars were stuck over Sonora Pass due to accumulating hail a few inches deep at approximately 16 June 1530PST. The pass was closed overnight due to the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Great Basin brought high winds and single digit relative humidity to southern California. Gusty Santa Ana winds were reported in the mountains and passes, with peak gusts topping out 60-70 mph. The windiest location was Fremont Canyon at 73 mph. A few small wildfires broke out across the region. The Water Fire was the smallest, but most destructive fire during this time period, destroying 3 structures and 2 vehicles. The 46 Fire charred 300 acres and prompted the evacuations of approximately 3600 residents, with no structures destroyed or injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sill Hill recorded a peak wind gust of 59 knots around 0800 PST on 10/30.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hillside fire erupted at 140 am along Highway 18. This wildfire destroyed 3 homes and damage 6 others. Up to 1300 persons were evacuated. Neighbors went door to door at 2 am to wake up people. The fire consumed about 250 acres near Muscoy in San Bernardino. The fire burned downhill due to 40-50 mph wind gusts (Santa Ana) in lower Waterman Canyon. This wildfire rescue made the A&E Network Rescue series as #1 in 2019. 1 fire fighter was hospitalized for smoke inhalation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge over the Great Basin brought offshore flow and hot temperatures to Southern California June 9th through 12th. The Coachella Valley and San Diego County deserts were the warmest locations with highs 110-117. In the Inland Empire, highs ranged from 105-110. A few locations in the San Diego inland areas (Ramona, Escondido, Alpine) reached the mid to upper 90s during the 4-day heatwave. Many locations broke daily high temperature records; most notably, El Cajon broke its daily record on June 10th by 10 degrees. In San Diego County, a total of 36 people were treated for heat-related illnesses between June 9th and 12th.||Dense fog was observed at the coast and on higher coastal terrain on June 12th with very low visibilities (less than 1/8 of a mile at times). Fog lifted and visibility improved by 9 AM on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Diego County deserts observed high temperatures ranging from 105-115 during the 4-day heatwave. Campo and Borrego Springs broke their daily high temperature records on multiple days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level low passed through the region, which brought elevated fire weather concerns to northern California. Areas impacted within Sacramento's forecast area include the Sacramento and San Joaquin Valleys for areas generally below 1000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On June 8, conditions were favorable for wildfire growth as relative humidity values fell into the teens and northerly winds gusted up to 25-45 mph. Prior to the start of this event, the local utility company decided to de-energize power lines per CA Public Utility Commissions (CPUC) Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) guidelines. Eventually, a fire did start at 1350PST in Yolo County, which became known as the Sand Fire. There were 2512 acres of land burned over a period of 8 days, along with 7 structures destroyed. The fire also led to road closures from Highway 16 to Highway 20 to the town Brooks in addition to the evacuation of residents living along County Road 41.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered monsoon thunderstorms developed across the high terrain of Joshua Tree National Park during the afternoon hours on July 23rd. Some of the stronger storms generated locally heavy rains with peak rain rates in excess of 1 inch per hour. The heavy rain resulted in an episode of flash flooding in the park. At 1350PST the National Park Service reported flash flooding along Pinto Basin Road. The flooding resulted in the need to clean debris off of the road surface from Milepost 17 to 21. No accidents resulted from the debris on the road. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued and was in effect at the time of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered monsoon thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside County including Joshua Tree National Park during the afternoon hours on July 23rd. Some of the stronger storms produced locally heavy rains with peak rain rates in excess of 1 inch per hour. The heavy rain led to an episode of flash flooding within the park. At 1350PST the National Park Service reported that flash flooding had occurred along  Pinto Basin Road, and as a result debris needed to be cleaned off of the road surface between Milepost 17 and 21. Fortunately no accidents resulted from the debris across the road. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued shortly before the reports of the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered monsoon thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside County during the afternoon hours on July 25th. Some of the stronger storms produced locally heavy rains that were sufficient to cause an episode of flash flooding to the east of Joshua Tree National Park. According to the California Highway Patrol, flash flooding caused long stretches of highway 62 to become closed. The report from the highway patrol came at 1626PST. The flooding was reported about 22 miles to the northeast of Eagle Mountain. No reports of accidents were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered monsoon thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside county during the afternoon hours on July 25th. The stronger storms generated heavy rain with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain was sufficient to produce an episode of flash flooding across the far north portion of the County. According to the California Highway Patrol, at 1626PST flash flooding resulted in a long stretch of highway 62 being closed to the east of Joshua Tree National Park. The flash flooding occurred about 22 miles to the northeast of Eagle Mountain. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued at 1540PST and was in effect at the time of the flooding. No reports of accidents or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northerly winds developed over the North and East Bay Hills. This along with a very dry airmass over the region, created critical fire weather conditions for the greater Bay Area. Scattered wildfires broke out across Northern California on Thursday the 10th at least one of which prompted the issuance of evacuations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small wildfire broke out in the hills near Moraga overnight prompting evacuations for nearby homes. Ultimately no structures were destroyed https://www.eastbaytimes.com/2019/10/10/fire-crews-battle-merrill-fire-in-moraga/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of offshore wind events plagued much of California towards the end of October. Cut off lows (also known as insider sliders) moved into the Great Basin as an upper ridge sat over the eastern Pacific. Strong surface high pressure also building over the Great Basin and a trough along the California coast provided the set up for strong and dry offshore winds over the greater Bay Area. Two more such events would go on to occur before the end of the month providing what would be historic critical fire weather conditions for the region. The first event brought strong N to NE winds to the region, particularly the North Bay, where gusts of 50 to 70 mph were observed. Healdsburg Hills North Station had a peak gust of 76 mph the night of the 23rd. These conditions fed the rapid growth of the Kincade Fire that broke out late in the evening on the 23rd, and at the end of the month was still burning. Additionally, near record breaking high temperatures were observed in parts of the area on the 24th and 25th. Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to  roughly 1 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near record breaking heat along with smoke from the Kincade Fire brought poor air quality to the Bay Area https://sanfrancisco.cbslocal.com/2019/10/25/poor-bay-area-air-quality-expected-because-of-heat-kincade-fire/|https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2019-10-28/air-quality-getty-fire-california-smoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dipped into central California, which brought rain and even some isolated snow showers to the higher elevation Sierra. Snow accumulation was minimal and mainly south of highway 50. Elsewhere, fuels were still primed for fire growth if ignited The incoming threat of gusty northerly and easterly winds and poor daytime humidity (15-25%) led to a Red Flag Warning. In the end, no major fires broke out from this event, but a utility company did suffer from wind and vegetation related damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Redding ASOS station reported a 45 mph wind gust. Gusty northerly winds were observed across the zone and caused damage. The local utility in Shasta County reported tree and wind related damages and hazards to power distribution equipment in the Cedar Creek, Girvan, Jessup, and Volta areas. The timing for this event is based on the Redding ASOS report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure dominated the weather over Northern California which brought above normal temperatures to the region on July 24th and beyond. Rivers during this time were still fast and cold moving in the foothills and mountains with water temperatures spanning 48 to 60 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 24th at 1700 PST three people were swimming in the Feather River Canyon when suddenly they started to be carried away by the current. Two of the swimmers escaped; however, the third person, a 9 year old child, did not. His body was located around 2000 PST under the Tobin Bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures in this zone ranged from 22 to 39 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow warming trend took place across the area between October 12 and October 16 with high pressure in place along the west coast. The high broke down on October 17 as a dry cold front pushed through the area. As the colder air pushed into the area during the afternoon and evening of October 17, strong winds impacted the mountains and deserts of Kern County with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 55 mph with a few stations topping out around 70 mph. The winds diminished during the morning of October 18 as the frontal system moved out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Peak RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of offshore wind events plagued much of California towards the end of October. Cut off lows (also known as insider sliders) moved into the Great Basin as an upper ridge sat over the eastern Pacific. Strong surface high pressure also building over the Great Basin and a trough along the California coast provided the set up for one of the strongest dry offshore winds over the greater Bay Area since the 2017 North Bay Fires. Winds remained elevated for at least 24 hours with gusts in the hills 60 to 80 mph. A peak gust of 102 mph was recorded near the Kincade Fire. These winds promoted rapid growth of the Kincade Fire and along with very dry conditions allowed for multiple new wildfires to spark in the greater Bay Area. A large amount of the community in Sonoma County was evacuated downwind of the Kincade Fire in response. Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to over 2 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wildfires broke out into Sunday morning around the region. One of them (Cypress Complex) was sparked early Sunday morning prompting the issuance of limited evacuations of nearby neighborhoods. Ultimately no structures were destroyed https://www.fire.ca.gov/incidents/2019/10/27/cypress-complex/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and final in a series of offshore wind events arrived at the end of October resulting in another round of Red Flag Warnings and Wind Advisories. This final event was not as strong as the previous and was more in line with the first offshore event that sparked the Kincade Fire. Under a very dry air mass widespread single digit humidity values were observed in the hills, along valley floors, and in some coastal areas. The Oakland Airport sounding also recorded record low moisture content for several launches in a row. For full detailed summary of these three events see the following site: https://www.weather.gov/mtr/FireWeatherOctober2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 4.8 miles SW of Anderson Springs recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over Arizona and Southern California resulted in dense fog on the coast and in the western valleys of San Diego County in the mornings on July 15th and 16th. Visibility reached less than 1/4 mile at times, especially on higher coastal terrain. Dense fog occurred again the morning of the 16th, with similar visibility restrictions. In Escondido, visibility was almost 0 for around 20-30 minutes before improving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brown Field ASOS reported dense fog with visibility between 1/4 and 1 mile from 0000 to 0600 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the eastern Pacific Ocean and the Gulf of California moved northward around the periphery of high pressure centered over the Four-Corners region of the Rockies the 24th into the 25th. This moisture, coupled with the weak flow and increased instability in the lower to middle portions of the atmosphere, resulted in storms producing heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mechanic working for a California Department of Transportation contractor was|struck by lightning on the afternoon of 25 July in Mono County. The incident happened at 1515PST at a maintenance yard at Sonora Junction. The mechanic had exited his pickup truck and lightning hit the truck and him. His injuries were not life-threatening according to the news report.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Great Basin brought moderate Santa Ana winds to southern California October 10th and 11th. Peak wind gusts of 60-65 mph were observed in the Cajon Pass, San Bernardino, Fontana and Fremont Canyon. Peak wind gusts of 55 mph were recorded at Cuyamaca Lake and the I-8 pass in San Diego County. Most of San Diego County had wind gusts of 35-45 mph. Humidity values were in the single digits throughout much of the region. Four wildfires started during this event - two of which were impactful. The Sandalwood Fire destroyed 75 mobile homes in Redlands and the Reche Fire caused evacuations in Moreno Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandalwood Fire started the morning of October 10th and resulted from a trash truck dumping burning garbage onto vegetation. A mobile home park was evacuated. A total of 16 structures were damaged and 74 structures were destroyed. Two people died as a result of this fire. The fire was fully contained on 10/14 with a final acreage of 1,011 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over the Great Basin ushered in gusty Santa Ana winds, hot temperatures and single digit relative humidity. Many SoCal locations took the nation's high temperature between October 21-24. Although it was hot and dry October 21-22, winds were not very strong. The stronger winds occurred on October 24-25, where widespread wind gusts of 50-60 mph winds occurred in the mountains and passes, with isolated gusts of 70+ mph. The highest wind gust was 78 mph at Sill Hill in San Diego County. Five small fires started between October 24-25. The Miller Fire in Valley Center burned 70 acres in heavy vegetation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sill Hill recorded a peak wind gust of 68 knots around 0700 PST on 10/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool airmass settled into the area between October 18 and October 20 with temperatures slightly below normal at most locations. A warming trend began on October 21 as high pressure moved inland. The high strengthened over the region through October 25 and kept a very warm and dry airmass over the area through October 26. A cold upper low pressure system dropped southward through the Great Basin on October 27 which brought some light rain and snow to the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada as well as much cooler temperatures and a period of increased winds with locally strong gusts. Several stations in the San Joaquin Valley and central Sierra reported winds gusts between 35 and 45 mph. A few locations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured gusts exceeding 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laural Mountain RAWS measures a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

Strong surface high pressure also building over the Great Basin and a trough along the California coast provided the set up for one of the strongest dry offshore winds over the greater Bay Area since the 2017 North Bay Fires. Winds remained elevated for at least 24 hours with gusts in the hills 60 to 80 mph. A peak gust of 102 mph was recorded near the Kincade Fire. These winds promoted rapid growth of the Kincade Fire and along with very dry conditions allowed for multiple new wildfires to spark in the greater Bay Area. A large amount of the community in Sonoma County was evacuated downwind of the Kincade Fire in response. Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to over 2 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 4.5 miles SW of Forest Lake recorded a maximum wind gust of 102 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of offshore wind events plagued much of California towards the end of October. Cut off lows (also known as insider sliders) moved into the Great Basin as an upper ridge sat over the eastern Pacific. Strong surface high pressure also building over the Great Basin and a trough along the California coast provided the set up for strong and dry offshore winds over the greater Bay Area. Two more such events would go on to occur before the end of the month providing what would be historic critical fire weather conditions for the region. The first event brought strong N to NE winds to the region, particularly the North Bay, where gusts of 50 to 70 mph were observed. Healdsburg Hills North Station had a peak gust of 76 mph the night of the 23rd. These conditions fed the rapid growth of the Kincade Fire that broke out late in the evening on the 23rd, and at the end of the month was still burning. Additionally, near record breaking high temperatures were observed in parts of the area on the 24th and 25th. Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to  roughly 1 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kincade Fire broke out on the night of Oct 23rd. GOES-17 satellite imagery detected the fire at approximately 9:21 PM PDT. The fire burned through early November. At the end of October the Fire burned 76,825 acres, 266 structures were destroyed, 47 were damaged, and 4 first responders were injured with one injury deemed serious https://www.fire.ca.gov/incidents/2019/10/23/kincade-fire/. The fire prompted numerous emergency evacuations throughout Sonoma County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot weather impacted the Coachella Valley, where high temperatures ranged from 110-116 degrees. The hottest day was July 12th, where both Palm Springs and Thermal reached 116 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over Arizona and Southern California resulted in dense fog on the coast and in the western valleys of San Diego County in the mornings on July 15th and 16th. Visibility reached less than 1/4 mile at times, especially on higher coastal terrain. Dense fog occurred again the morning of the 16th, with similar visibility restrictions. In Escondido, visibility was almost 0 for around 20-30 minutes before improving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palomar Airport reported dense fog with visibility of less than 1/4 mile to 1/4 mile from 0100 to 0600 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over the Great Basin ushered in gusty Santa Ana winds, hot temperatures and single digit relative humidity. Many SoCal locations took the nation's high temperature between October 21-24. Although it was hot and dry October 21-22, winds were not very strong. The stronger winds occurred on October 24-25, where widespread wind gusts of 50-60 mph winds occurred in the mountains and passes, with isolated gusts of 70+ mph. The highest wind gust was 78 mph at Sill Hill in San Diego County. Five small fires started between October 24-25. The Miller Fire in Valley Center burned 70 acres in heavy vegetation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highland Springs recorded a peak wind gust of 67 knots around 0800 PST on 10/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Building high pressure and offshore flow caused a rapid warm-up across the Bay Area prompting two days worth of heat advisories along with several daily record high temperatures. Stronger offshore flow compared to a mid-September heat wave also created increased fire danger and the issuance of a Red Flag Warning. Heat related power failure in the North Bay left several thousand customers without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat related power failure in northwest Santa Rosa on Wednesday September 25th left 5,500 customers without power https://www.pressdemocrat.com/news/10096356-181/power-cut-off-to-5500?sba=AAS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low pressure system, combined with remnant tropical cyclone moisture, brought heavy rain and mountain snow to southern California. Across the area, 1 to 4 inches of rain fell, which was heaviest on the 19th and 20th. Rainfall rates of 0.50 to 1 inch per hour observed which caused impacts to many areas including Valley Center, Alpine and the Mexico border. A waterspout was spotted (photo) just west of San Clemente and Dana Point (LA waters) on the 20th, with another possible waterspout sighting on the 21st. Snow was observed down to 4200 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many locations in the San Bernardino Mountains reported over 5 inches of snow over 48 hours. Green Valley Lake was the highest with 13 inches of snow. Arrowbear and Running Springs reported 9 inches of snow. Big Bear resorts and Mount Baldy reported 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold unstable upper level low pressure system from the northwest brought widespread heavy rain, thunderstorms and mountain snow to southern California over the Thanksgiving holiday. One to three feet of snow fell above 5,500 feet in the mountains, impacting highways and passes. Big Bear and Mountain High reported 30 to 48 inches total. Highway 18 was closed through December 1st. Over 40 vehicles were stuck on Highway 74 and 371, while 20 vehicles were abandoned on Highway 38. There were 10 car accidents involving snow plows. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 3 inches fell at the coast and in the valleys. San Diego River reached 9.5 feet with flooding. Numerous roadways were flooded out, especially on November 28th when the heaviest rain fell. An RV park in La Mesa flooded out due to flash flooding. The San Diego River at Fashion Valley reached 9.5 feet. 2 separate episodes of severe thunderstorms occurred in San Diego County on Thanksgiving Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and gusty winds knocked down a tree and nearby power line in Crestline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the eastern Pacific Ocean and the Gulf of California moved northward around the periphery of high pressure centered over the Four-Corners region of the Rockies the 24th into the 25th. This moisture, coupled with the weak flow and increased instability in the lower to middle portions of the atmosphere, resulted in storms producing heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS, 3 miles east of Coleville (elevation 5440 feet) reported 0.54 inches of rainfall in a 4-hour period from 25 July 1448PST to 1848PST.

Rainfall rates of 0.50 to 1 inch per hour observed which caused impacts to many areas including Valley Center, Alpine and the Mexico border. A waterspout was spotted (photo) just west of San Clemente and Dana Point (LA waters) on the 20th, with another possible waterspout sighting on the 21st. Snow was observed down to 4200 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported 12 San Diego State University dorm rooms flooded from heavy rain on the evening of the 20th. Water was entering the first floor of the dorm room.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold unstable upper level low pressure system from the northwest brought widespread heavy rain, thunderstorms and mountain snow to southern California over the Thanksgiving holiday. One to three feet of snow fell above 5,500 feet in the mountains, impacting highways and passes. Big Bear and Mountain High reported 30 to 48 inches total. Highway 18 was closed through December 1st. Over 40 vehicles were stuck on Highway 74 and 371, while 20 vehicles were abandoned on Highway 38. There were 10 car accidents involving snow plows. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 3 inches fell at the coast and in the valleys. San Diego River reached 9.5 feet with flooding. Numerous roadways were flooded out, especially on November 28th when the heaviest rain fell. An RV park in La Mesa flooded out due to flash flooding. The San Diego River at Fashion Valley reached 9.5 feet. 2 separate episodes of severe thunderstorms occurred in San Diego County on Thanksgiving Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Motorist struck a 500 pound boulder on state highway 74 Ortega highway through the mountain pass. Heavy rainfall occurred the entire day which likely caused the debris flow and large boulder to be released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2019 began on the cool side with below normal temperatures during the first five days of the month. The 6th through 8th were much warmer as temperatures rose to above seasonal normals with high pressure prevailing over the area. A dry cold front then pushed southward through central California on October 9 which brought much cooler temperatures to the area as daytime high plunged by 10 to 15 degrees at most locations as well as with a period of strong winds across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts. While most locations had peak wind gusts between 35 and 50 mph a few low impact indicator sites measured stronger gusts. Behind the front on October 10, a dry airmass settled into the area lowering relative humidity into the single digits as offshore easterly winds developed. This resulted in elevated fire danger across the mountains in Kern County from the late morning of October 10 to the afternoon of October 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 83 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of offshore wind events plagued much of California towards the end of October. Cut off lows (also known as insider sliders) moved into the Great Basin as an upper ridge sat over the eastern Pacific. Strong surface high pressure also building over the Great Basin and a trough along the California coast provided the set up for one of the strongest dry offshore winds over the greater Bay Area since the 2017 North Bay Fires. Winds remained elevated for at least 24 hours with gusts in the hills 60 to 80 mph. A peak gust of 102 mph was recorded near the Kincade Fire. These winds promoted rapid growth of the Kincade Fire and along with very dry conditions allowed for multiple new wildfires to spark in the greater Bay Area. A large amount of the community in Sonoma County was evacuated downwind of the Kincade Fire in response. Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to over 2 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine people were injured when winds knocked down a 30 foot tall tree in Martinez. Six people were taken to the hospital with non-life-threatening injuries https://sanfrancisco.cbslocal.com/2019/10/27/halloween-9-injured-at-martinez-farmers-market-after-high-winds-knock-down-tree/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over Southern California brought hot temperatures to the inland areas. 850 millibar temperatures ranged from 28-30 degrees Celsius over the eastern portion of the region. High temperatures in the Coachella Valley ranged from 113-118, where Palm Springs reached 118 degrees. The Inland Empire experienced hot weather too, with high temperatures ranging from 97-111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in the Inland Empire ranged from 97-111 degrees on both July 27th and 28th, with the highest temperature of 111 degrees in Rancho Cucamonga.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over the Great Basin ushered in gusty Santa Ana winds, hot temperatures and single digit relative humidity. Many SoCal locations took the nation's high temperature between October 21-24. Although it was hot and dry October 21-22, winds were not very strong. The stronger winds occurred on October 24-25, where widespread wind gusts of 50-60 mph winds occurred in the mountains and passes, with isolated gusts of 70+ mph. The highest wind gust was 78 mph at Sill Hill in San Diego County. Five small fires started between October 24-25. The Miller Fire in Valley Center burned 70 acres in heavy vegetation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures resulted from Santa Ana winds on October 22nd in the Inland Empire. Ontario reached 95, Chino reached 98 and San Bernardino reached 97.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Great Basin brought high winds and single digit relative humidity to southern California. Gusty Santa Ana winds were reported in the mountains and passes, with peak gusts topping out 60-70 mph. The windiest location was Fremont Canyon at 73 mph. A few small wildfires broke out across the region. The Water Fire was the smallest, but most destructive fire during this time period, destroying 3 structures and 2 vehicles. The 46 Fire charred 300 acres and prompted the evacuations of approximately 3600 residents, with no structures destroyed or injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hill fire broke out in Jurupa Valley at 10 am. The fire burned just 600 acres. 1 reported related injury. Evacuations were lifted by the end of the day. Santa Ana winds were occurring. The fire caused the closure of major Highway 60.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of offshore wind events plagued much of California towards the end of October. Cut off lows (also known as insider sliders) moved into the Great Basin as an upper ridge sat over the eastern Pacific. Strong surface high pressure also building over the Great Basin and a trough along the California coast provided the set up for strong and dry offshore winds over the greater Bay Area. Two more such events would go on to occur before the end of the month providing what would be historic critical fire weather conditions for the region. The first event brought strong N to NE winds to the region, particularly the North Bay, where gusts of 50 to 70 mph were observed. Healdsburg Hills North Station had a peak gust of 76 mph the night of the 23rd. These conditions fed the rapid growth of the Kincade Fire that broke out late in the evening on the 23rd, and at the end of the month was still burning. Additionally, near record breaking high temperatures were observed in parts of the area on the 24th and 25th. Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to  roughly 1 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Healdsburg Hills North Station reported a wind gust of 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively vigorous upper-level trough impacted the Pacific Northwest during mid September. Cold air aloft associated with the upper trough yielded an unstable airmass supportive of strong showers and a few thunderstorms. Training showers and storms produced heavy rainfall near Humboldt Bay resulting in localized urban flash flooding in and around the city of Arcata.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixteen buildings at Humbolt State University suffered minor damage due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled into the area on October 30. As winds diminished during the morning of October 31 under clear skies, strong radiational cooling took place and minimum temperatures plunged into the lower to mid 30's in the San Joaquin Valley and into the lower to mid 20's across the Kern County Deserts resulting in the first hard freeze of the season for the Kern County Deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 19 and 27 degrees.

Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to over 2 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Los Vaqueros RAWS station recorded wind gusts between 45 and 60 mph throughout this time frame with sustained winds around 35 to 40 mph. A peak wind gust of 70 mph was recorded at 3:45 pm (PDT) that afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively vigorous upper-level trough impacted the Pacific Northwest during mid September. Cold air aloft associated with the upper trough yielded an unstable airmass supportive of strong showers and a few thunderstorms. Training showers and storms produced heavy rainfall near Humboldt Bay resulting in localized urban flash flooding in and around the city of Arcata.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Lewis Avenue and Daina Court was flooded.

CZ_NAME: EAST BAY HILLS AND THE DIABLO RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of offshore wind events plagued much of California towards the end of October. Cut off lows (also known as insider sliders) moved into the Great Basin as an upper ridge sat over the eastern Pacific. Strong surface high pressure also building over the Great Basin and a trough along the California coast provided the set up for strong and dry offshore winds over the greater Bay Area. Two more such events would go on to occur before the end of the month providing what would be historic critical fire weather conditions for the region. The first event brought strong N to NE winds to the region, particularly the North Bay, where gusts of 50 to 70 mph were observed. Healdsburg Hills North Station had a peak gust of 76 mph the night of the 23rd. These conditions fed the rapid growth of the Kincade Fire that broke out late in the evening on the 23rd, and at the end of the month was still burning. Additionally, near record breaking high temperatures were observed in parts of the area on the 24th and 25th. Prior to the event PG&E shut off power to  roughly 1 million people across the state of California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oakland North RAWS reporting wind gusts of around 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled into the area on October 30. As winds diminished during the morning of October 31 under clear skies, strong radiational cooling took place and minimum temperatures plunged into the lower to mid 30's in the San Joaquin Valley and into the lower to mid 20's across the Kern County Deserts resulting in the first hard freeze of the season for the Kern County Deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 19 and 27 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure strengthened over the four corners region on July 24 and 25 and resulted in widespread triple digit heat across the San Joaquin Valley. The high then built westward into central California between July 26 and 28. This resulted in a continued warming trend across the area between July 26 and July 28. The 28th which was the warmest day before the high broke down and gave way to a cooling trend which ended the widespread triple digit heat by July 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 102 and 107 degrees at most locations between July 25 and July 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of September, little to no rainfall fell on areas of the northern Sierra and mountains west of Susanville. Dry and warm conditions also persisted. A large wildfire started during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Fire burned in Genesee Valley in the Plumas National Forest east of Taylorsville from the afternoon of the 4th through the 25th. Smoke from this fire affected parts of western Nevada and northeast California, with the most widespread smoke reaching the Reno Metropolitan area on the 8th. This fire would eventually burn 54,612 acres before heavy rain on the 16th ended nearly all remaining fire activity, with full containment achieved on the 25th. Several evacuations were ordered for the Genesee Valley corridor, the Ward Creek area, the Flournoy Bridge area, and Antelope Lake. According to the Plumas National Forest, 9 structures were destroyed in the fire and the cost was estimated to be near $35,100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high set up over the Four Corners region, allowing moisture to move into Southern California. Showers set up over the coast and valleys on July 22nd, and inland thunderstorms developed in the afternoon. On July 23rd, a severe thunderstorm set up over the Coachella Valley and brought 70 mph winds to Mecca. This resulted in over 8 million dollars in damages to power lines throughout the city. On July 24th, storms prompted flash flood warnings over the Riverside and San Diego County mountains. On July 25th, strong thunderstorms over Julian and Borrego Springs brought gusty winds, small hail and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported flooding in their backyard from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold unstable upper level low pressure system from the northwest brought widespread heavy rain, thunderstorms and mountain snow to southern California over the Thanksgiving holiday. One to three feet of snow fell above 5,500 feet in the mountains, impacting highways and passes. Big Bear and Mountain High reported 30 to 48 inches total. Highway 18 was closed through December 1st. Over 40 vehicles were stuck on Highway 74 and 371, while 20 vehicles were abandoned on Highway 38. There were 10 car accidents involving snow plows. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 3 inches fell at the coast and in the valleys. San Diego River reached 9.5 feet with flooding. Numerous roadways were flooded out, especially on November 28th when the heaviest rain fell. An RV park in La Mesa flooded out due to flash flooding. The San Diego River at Fashion Valley reached 9.5 feet. 2 separate episodes of severe thunderstorms occurred in San Diego County on Thanksgiving Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees fell and snapped at the Wildlands Conservancy in Oak Glen due to heavy, wet snow. The area received around 18 to 24 inches of snow from the storm. The hundreds of downed trees resulted in closed roads and trails as well as cut off power to the area. An estimated 2 million dollars in damage occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 106 and 111 degrees at most locations on July 28 and July 29.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS SOUTHWEST SHASTA COUNTY TO NORTHERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low with embedded disturbances brought isolated showers and thunderstorms to the region. Model data indicated that any available moisture would be|above 700 millibars so rainfall with any thunderstorms would be limited. These conditions, combined with seasonably dry fuels and moderate to poor humidity recoveries, led to fire weather concerns for northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On September 5 thunderstorms developed over interior northern California sparking several wildfires within the region. This particular fire was named the South Fire and it grew up to 5,332 acres, with the majority of the fire growth occurring between September 5-21. Terrain is steep and rugged out there, which made containment difficult and called for road closures at times. The last major update that the Shasta-Trinity National Forest Service put out was on September 21, 2019; however, on November 27, 2019 at 10AM PST, they reported the fire was officially 100% contained and controlled.

Tropical moisture pushed up into central California along the west side of a large high pressure center which was positioned over the four corners region on July 24 and July 25. As the atmosphere destabilized in the afternoon, thunderstorms developed over the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada each day. While the thunderstorms on the afternoon of the July 24 were localized for the most part, measurable precipitation was more widespread on the afternoon of July 25 when scattered thunderstorms developed over the mountains. Some of these thunderstorms drifted westward into the Sierra foothills and the San Joaquin Valley. A few of the thunderstorms in the Sierra Nevada produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding was observed at Tuolumne Meadows in Yosemite National Park. While amounts at most locations were generally a tenth of an inch of inch of less, a few locations in the Central Sierra in Tuolumne and Madera Counties picked up over half an inch of rainfall in three hours or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yosemite Park Rangers reported flash flooding near the Dana Creek and State Route 120 intersection at Tuolumne Meadows where 40 people were rescued when the river swelled and separated them from the highway.

This resulted in widespread triple digit heat across the San Joaquin Valley, southern Sierra foothills and Kern County Deserts during this time frame. Excessive heat highlights were issued for the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills as a result. A cooling trend then took place on August 16 and 17 as an upper trough pushes into the Pacific Northwest and broke down the large area of high pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 103 and 108 degrees at most locations between August 14 and August 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed along the coast and extended into most valley areas on the morning of August 20th, with visibility as low as 1/8 of a mile at times. Fog lifted and visibility improved after 0800 PST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog observed in Escondido on 8/20 at 6:30 am with visibility of 1/8 mile. Fog lifted and visibility improved by 0800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave developed August 2-4 with high temperatures 98 to 102F for Inland Empire and 115F at Palm Springs on Sunday August 2. The heat peaked on August 5-6, 2019. The impact was mainly the Inland Empire and Coachella Valley. On August 5 and 6, the Inland Empire cities reached 103 to 107 both days and on Monday August 5, Palm Springs recorded its hottest temperature of the year at 121F. Ocotillo Wells and Thermal were both 120F. Palm Springs was 116F on Sunday August 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Springs reach 115F on August 2, 116F on August 4 and the hottest day of the summer at 121F on August 5. On August 5 Thermal and Ocotillo Wells reached 120F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure dominated over the region between August 25 and 27. Temperatures across the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills nudged upward back into the triple digits by August 25 and prompted the issuance of a Heat Advisory for the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills by the 27th as well as an Excessive Heat Warning for the Kern County Deserts where highs approached 110 degrees. The high slowly broke down on August 28 and 29 giving way to a cooling trend across the area although temperatures remained above seasonal normals for late August as they were for much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 101 and 106 degrees at most locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms moved across the area on 9/5/19.  Dozens of fires were initiated by lightning.  One of them was the Lone Wildfire, which was started by a lightning strike at 05/1614 PDT.  As of the last report at 17/0430 PDT, the fire was at 5737 acres and was 100 percent contained.  0 structures were lost and 1.6 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought extensive heat to the region from August 14 to August 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 15th a wildfire was reported in the community of Millville in Shasta County. This fire burned a 156 acres over a period of two days during a brief heatwave over northern California. On Calfire's web page for the Cottage Fire, it notes that one fire fighter was injured due to heat related illness. An Excessive Heat Warning was in place from 1100 August 14 to 2100 August 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought hot and dry weather to northern California from August 23 through August 26, 2019. Temperatures were forecast to be 5 to 10 degrees above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CalFire reported that a wildfire occurred on the morning of August 22 and lasted through the evening of August 26. This fire was named the Mountain Fire and burned 600 acres of land. It damaged 5 residential buildings and 2 additional structures in addition to destroying 7 residential buildings and 7 other structures.  A local news paper also reported that about 14 wooden power poles were damaged and that the local power company worked actively to replace them. CalFire also noted that there were 3 cases of minor injuries, and that all the victims were treated and released. There were numerous evacuations, around 3,885 residents, and road closures within the area which were all lifted on the by the evening of August 24.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amboy Road was flooded, and vehicles were pulling off the road due to poor visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river associated with a midlatitude cyclone brought greater than 2 inches of rainfall to the Sierra Nevada and 1 to 2 feet of snowfall above 7000 feet in the Lake Tahoe Basin and in Mono County. Rainfall totals in northeast California and the lower valleys of northern Mono County ranged from around 0.50 inches up to nearly 1.50 inches. Freezing levels derived from WFO Reno soundings rose slowly throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HADS station ERYC1 at Ellery Lake reported 1.70 inches of rainfall in a 48-hour period from 1 December 0000PST to 2 December 2359PST. Station elevation of 9504 ft MSL.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An uppper level high centered over western Arizona ushered in hot weather to parts of Southern California. The deserts and inland valleys experienced much warmer than normal temperatures, with August 14th and 15th being the hottest days of the event. Temperatures were hottest in the Coachella Valley, where Thermal reached 120 degrees and Palm Springs reached 119 on August 15th. Ramona reached 100 degrees and Lake Elsinore reached 105 degrees on August 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5 acre vegetation fire broke out at Bay Ave. and Phyllis Ave. in Moreno Valley late on August 15th. 2 single family homes were destroyed, as well as 3 outbuildings and 9 vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late evening monsoon thunderstorms developed across far eastern Riverside County on September 1st, and some of the stronger storms generated locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates that exceeded one inch per hour. The heavy rains led to episodes of flash flooding along the lower Colorado River Valley north of Blythe. The flash flooding primarily affected motorists driving along Highway 95. According to a report from the department of highways, 7 to 8 vehicles became stuck in flooded portions of Highway 95 about 15 miles northeast of Midland. In addition, the highway was closed at Wind River Road due to flash flooding. The flash flooding occurred after 2100PST. There were no reports of injuries due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms developed across far eastern Riverside County during the evening hours on September 1st and some of the stronger storms generated locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain led to some flash flooding, affecting the lower Colorado River Valley and Highway 95 north of the town of Blythe. According to the Department of Highways, or Caltrans, at 2139PST flash flooding 15 miles east of Midland resulted in the closure of Highway 95 at Wind River Road. No accidents were reported due to the flooding. A Flash Flood Warning was issued shortly thereafter, at 2210PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late evening monsoon thunderstorms developed across far eastern Riverside County on September 1st, and some of the stronger storms generated locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates that exceeded one inch per hour. The heavy rains led to episodes of flash flooding along the lower Colorado River Valley north of Blythe. The flash flooding primarily affected motorists driving along Highway 95. According to a report from the department of highways, 7 to 8 vehicles became stuck in flooded portions of Highway 95 about 15 miles northeast of Midland. In addition, the highway was closed at Wind River Road due to flash flooding. The flash flooding occurred after 2100PST. There were no reports of injuries due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across the far eastern portions of Riverside County on September 1st and some of the stronger storms generated locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The very heavy rain led to some flash flooding that impacted the lower Colorado River Valley as well as Highway 95 north of the town of Blythe. According to a report from Caltrans, or the department of highways, at 2235PST flash flooding caused 7 to 8 vehicles to become stuck between flooded portions of US Highway 95 about 15 miles northeast of Midland. Fortunately there were no injuries reported. A Flash Flood Warning was in effect at the time of the flooding, and was issued at 2210PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An uppper level high centered over western Arizona ushered in hot weather to parts of Southern California. The deserts and inland valleys experienced much warmer than normal temperatures, with August 14th and 15th being the hottest days of the event. Temperatures were hottest in the Coachella Valley, where Thermal reached 120 degrees and Palm Springs reached 119 on August 15th. Ramona reached 100 degrees and Lake Elsinore reached 105 degrees on August 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous locations in the Coachella Valley experienced extreme high temperatures on August 14th and 15th. Thermal Airport recorded a high temperature of 120 degrees, and Palm Springs Airport recorded a high temperature of 119 degrees on August 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered monsoon thunderstorms developed across the far eastern portion of Riverside County during the evening hours on September 1st. Some of the stronger storms generated gusty and damaging outflow wind in excess of 60 mph. One such storm in Rice Valley produced a 69 mph wind gust as measured by a RAWS weather station. At 2139PST strong winds downed power lines across Riverfront Road near the lower Colorado River Valley 14 miles northeast of Blythe. No injuries were reported due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms developed across the far northeast portion of Riverside County during the evening hours on September 1st and one of the stronger storms produced very gusty outflow winds.  A wind gust to 58 mph was recorded by the RAWS station RVYC1 in Rice Valley located to the south of Highway 62. No reports of damage were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered monsoon thunderstorms developed across the far eastern portion of Riverside County during the evening hours on September 1st. At 2139PST strong winds downed power lines across Riverfront Road near the lower Colorado River Valley 14 miles northeast of Blythe. No injuries were reported due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms developed across portions of far eastern Riverside County along the lower Colorado River Valley during the evening hours on September 1st. One of the stronger storms generated very gusty and damaging outflow winds estimated to be as high as 60 mph.  According to Caltrans, or the Department of Highways, gusty winds downed power lines across Riverfront Road about 14 miles northeast of Blythe and near Highway 95. No injuries were reported as a result of the downed lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure dominated over the region between August 25 and 27. Temperatures across the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills nudged upward back into the triple digits by August 25 and prompted the issuance of a Heat Advisory for the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills by the 27th as well as an Excessive Heat Warning for the Kern County Deserts where highs approached 110 degrees. The high slowly broke down on August 28 and 29 giving way to a cooling trend across the area although temperatures remained above seasonal normals for late August as they were for much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 104 and 109 degrees at most locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river associated with a large mid-latitude cyclone brought heavy rainfall and snowfall to the Sierra Nevada over the course of three days. Liquid totals were between 2 and 3 inches from central Lassen County south along the Sierra Crest west of Lake Tahoe and into Mono County, with 1 to 2 inches across much of the Lake Tahoe Basin and Alpine County. Snowfall totals were generally 4 inches or less across the Lake Tahoe Basin and northeast California below 5500 feet; 4 to 8 inches fell up to 7000 feet west of Lake Tahoe and in higher elevations of northeast California. In the Sierra above 7000 feet, snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 feet, with a few locations receiving up to 30 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS RWBC1 1 mile west-southwest of Meyers reported 2.15 inches of rainfall in a 48-hour period from 6 December 1751PST to 8 December 1751PST at an elevation of 6336 feet MSL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures, along with dry conditions, occurred the 23rd through the end of the month. Breezy conditions occurred on the 24th and 25th, but combined effects of winds and humidity did not reach critical fire weather criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Long Valley Fire started near the Red Rock exit along US Highway 395 and spread across the California/Nevada border into northwestern Nevada the 24th before being contained the 27th. The fire burned 2438 acres, and one structure was destroyed. The Federal Emergency Management Agency stated that the fire threatened 250-300 homes in and around Rancho Haven (Nevada). The fire also threatened five buildings, infrastructure, utilities, equipment, farms, ranches, and a watershed in the area. Mandatory evacuations were ordered for approximately 800 people before containment was reached. Firefighting costs were approximately $600K, according to the Nevada Bureau of Land Management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper high pressure area that was centered over the four corners region built westward into central California between August 14 and 16. This resulted in widespread triple digit heat across the San Joaquin Valley, southern Sierra foothills and Kern County Deserts during this time frame. Excessive heat highlights were issued for the San Joaquin Valley and southern Sierra foothills as a result. A cooling trend then took place on August 16 and 17 as an upper trough pushes into the Pacific Northwest and broke down the large area of high pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 103 and 108 degrees at most locations between August 14 and August 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the region on the 7th bringing gusty southerly winds, heavy rain, and flooding. Winds brought down trees and power lines across the greater Bay Area resulting in injuries to two people in San Francisco. Downed power lines also left over 1,000 people without power in the Santa Cruz Mountains as well as causing several additional power outages across San Francisco. A line of strong showers moved over San Francisco bringing urbanized flooding to the city and strong winds. Roads as well as  municipal stations were underwater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reporting mud/dirt/rock along CA Hwy 1 near Limekiln Campground. Reports of full closure of northbound and southbound traffic for another hour or so.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Great Basin brought moderate Santa Ana winds to southern California October 10th and 11th. Peak wind gusts of 60-65 mph were observed in the Cajon Pass, San Bernardino, Fontana and Fremont Canyon. Peak wind gusts of 55 mph were recorded at Cuyamaca Lake and the I-8 pass in San Diego County. Most of San Diego County had wind gusts of 35-45 mph. Humidity values were in the single digits throughout much of the region. Four wildfires started during this event - two of which were impactful. The Sandalwood Fire destroyed 75 mobile homes in Redlands and the Reche Fire caused evacuations in Moreno Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Reche Fire started on 10/10 and quickly grew to 350 acres near a residential area. This forced the evacuation of many homes in Moreno Valley. In total, the fire was 350 acres, damaged 1 structure and destroyed 1 structure. The fire was fully contained on 10/13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system along the far southern California coast spread considerable moisture inland and into far southeast California on Thursday December 26th. Strong forcing from the upper low combined with the moisture to produce an episode of flash flooding along the northeast shore of the Salton Sea during the morning hours. The California highway patrol reported that State Route 111 was closed due to the flash flooding. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued and was in effect at the time of the flooding. There were no reports of accidents or injuries due to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific low spread considerable moisture into far southeast California on December 26th; the moisture combined with modest instability and strong upward lift to produce widespread showers that impacted areas around the Salton Sea. The heavy rain even led to an episode of flash flooding on the northeast shore of the Salton Sea during the morning hours. According to a report from the California Highway Patrol, flash flooding caused State Route 111 to become closed at 1017PST. The closure was roughly 6 miles to the southwest of Imperial Hot Mine. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Great Basin brought moderate Santa Ana winds to southern California October 10th and 11th. Peak wind gusts of 60-65 mph were observed in the Cajon Pass, San Bernardino, Fontana and Fremont Canyon. Peak wind gusts of 55 mph were recorded at Cuyamaca Lake and the I-8 pass in San Diego County. Most of San Diego County had wind gusts of 35-45 mph. Humidity values were in the single digits throughout much of the region. Four wildfires started during this event - two of which were impactful. The Sandalwood Fire destroyed 75 mobile homes in Redlands and the Reche Fire caused evacuations in Moreno Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highland Springs recorded a peak wind gust of 53 knots around 0300 PST on 10/10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the lower deserts of far southeast California and far southwest Arizona during the afternoon hours on September 26th. Some of the stronger storms produced very heavy rain that led to an episode of flash flooding along the lower Colorado River valley between the towns of Parker and Blythe. During the middle of the afternoon a trained weather spotter 14 miles northeast of Blythe reported flash flooding along Highway 95. Flash flooding occurred in multiple locations with water moving over Highway 95 near mile marker 16, making the road impassable. A Flash Flood Warning was in effect at the time of the flooding. There were no reports of accidents or injuries due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along the lower Colorado River Valley during the afternoon hours on September 26th. Some of the stronger storms produced very heavy rain with peak rain rates over one inch per hour. The heavy rain led to an episode of flash flooding along Highway 95 between the towns of Blythe and Parker. According to a trained weather spotter, at 1444PST flash flooding was occurring in multiple spots about 14 miles north-northeast of Blythe. Water was seen running over Highway 95 near Mile Marker 16 making the road impassable. Fortunately there were no reports of any accidents due to the flash flooding and hazardous driving conditions.  A Flash Flood Warning was in effect at the time of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river associated with a large mid-latitude cyclone brought heavy rainfall and snowfall to the Sierra Nevada over the course of three days. Liquid totals were between 2 and 3 inches from central Lassen County south along the Sierra Crest west of Lake Tahoe and into Mono County, with 1 to 2 inches across much of the Lake Tahoe Basin and Alpine County. Snowfall totals were generally 4 inches or less across the Lake Tahoe Basin and northeast California below 5500 feet; 4 to 8 inches fell up to 7000 feet west of Lake Tahoe and in higher elevations of northeast California. In the Sierra above 7000 feet, snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 feet, with a few locations receiving up to 30 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained weather spotter 6 miles south-southeast of Susanville reported 3.00 inches of rainfall in a 48-hour period from 6 December 0800PST to 8 December 0800PST at an elevation of 4823 feet MSL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the lower deserts of Imperial County - from the Imperial Valley eastward to the lower Colorado River valley - during the afternoon and evening hours on September 25th. Instability was elevated due to the close proximity of an upper level low pressure system and cool air aloft, and the increased instability aided the thunderstorm development. As was the case over the past couple of days, the stronger thunderstorms primarily generated very heavy rain with peak rain rates well in excess of one inch per hour. Multiple reports from the California Highway Patrol indicated that flash flooding was occurring along State Route 78, from near the Cibola National Wildlife Refuge south to near Interstate 8. Local law enforcement also reported 30 vehicles stuck due to flowing water in a wash near Ogilby Road about 10 miles northeast of Glamis. Later in the evening, around 1945PST, there was a public report of flash flooding in Brawley where water at least one foot deep was overflowing an embankment. Multiple Flash Flood Warnings were issued; fortunately there were no reports of injuries due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the eastern portion of Imperial County during the afternoon hours on September 25th. Some of the stronger storms generated heavy rain, with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain led to some flash flooding along Ogilby Road between State Route 78 and Interstate 8. According to the California Highway Patrol, at 1449PST flash flooding along Ogilby Road resulted in the road becoming closed from Interstate 8 through State Route 78 due to a flooded wash covering the road in between those points. Due to the road closure, no vehicles were able to drive through the flooded wash and no accidents were reported. A Flash Flood Warning was issued, but for areas to the north of the flooded wash.
