EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry, cool air mass following a Santa Ana wind event lead to widespread frost and freeze in the valleys and coastal areas. Low temperatures in the valley areas ranged from the mid 20s to the mid 30s. Coastal areas had low temperatures in the low to mid 30s. Frost was observed in most valley areas and in some coastal areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frost was reported in Rancho Bernardo by an NWS employee. Low temperatures were in the low to mid 30s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems from the north and northwest, plus a weak tap into atmospheric river moisture, brought rain, mountain snow, and gusty winds to Southern California. Snow levels reached as low as 2000 feet, and brought high impact snow to locations above 4000 feet. Both I-8 through the San Diego Mountains and I-15 through the Cajon Pass were closed due to heavy snowfall. Between December 22-24, the coastal areas received 1-1.5 inches of rainfall, the valleys received 0.50-1 inch, and the mountain areas received up to 1 inch of rainfall. Again on December 26 through 27, coast and valley areas received 1 to 1.5 inches. Mountain areas received up to an inch. There were many water rescues, particularly in the Tijuana River Valley due to individuals driving through flooded roads and getting stuck. The San Diego River at Fashion Valley reached 10.5 feet on December 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snow from December 22 through 24 in the San Bernardino Mountains: Angelus Oaks 6 inches, Mountain Pass 1 foot, Arrowhead 25 inches, Big Bear City 3 feet, Wrightwood 8 inches, Top of Cajon Pass 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and very cold low pressure system dropped southward along the California coast on Christmas Day bringing some light precipitation inland into central California. The system then turned inland and moved through southern California during the evening of December 25 through the morning of December 26 bringing moderate to heavy precipitation to much of Kern County with lighter amounts further north. Between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation fell across much of Kern County with the snow level between 2500 and 3000 feet. Above the snow line, 1 to 2 feet of new snowfall was reported at several stations and the heavy snowfall resulted in the closure of several highways (including Interstate 5 south of Grapevine) for an extended period of time during a period of normally heavy holiday traffic after several vehicle either slid off of the highway or spun out. One truck driver was killed after being stranded along Interstate 5 for several hours. Across the Kern County Deserts and in the San Joaquin Valley from Bakersfield southward, heavy rainfall resulted in several roads being closed for a few hours with several reports of nuisance flooding reported by the Emergency Managers office or by law enforcement. There were also several reports of strong wind gusts and some reports of minor wind damage in the Fort Tejon and Tehachapi areas. Further north precipitation amounts were significantly lower although parts of Kings County picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall. The remainder of the area generally picked up a quarter of an inch or less of liquid precipitation before the system moved to the east of the area during the evening of December 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported rocks in the roadway on southbound SR 14 near Abbott Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving in from the Gulf of Alaska and drawing in moisture from the tropics combined to bring the first atmospheric river event of the winter season to the Greater Bay Area. This system brought widespread heavy rainfall, roadway flooding, and strong winds to the region. Rare Storm Warnings were issued over the coastal waters where buoys reported wind gusts in excess of 50 mph. Bay Area peaks recorded wind gusts between 60 to 70 mph with gusts along the Monterey Peninsula and Big Sur Coast at 50 to 60 mph. These winds caused downed trees and power outages across the area. Additionally, convection associated wit the main low caused severe flooding in the city of Chualar where homes were flooded. and cars were stranded on a flooded highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rocks in both lanes on River Rd near intersection with Rio Nido Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out and winds diminished following a weak system which produced some light rainfall across the Kern County Deserts during the evening hours on December 6. Patches of dense fog formed in the Indian Wells Valley for a few hours during the morning of December 7. The fog caused a fatal multi-vehicle accident along State Route 14 near Inyokern.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a multi-vehicle accident around 200 am PST on State Route 14 just south of Inyokern. An occupant of one of the vehicles was killed in the accident. Fog was reported in the area just before the time of the accident and lasted for around two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system slowly moved east across central California on December 22 and 23. Strong winds ahead of the system impacted the Fort Tejon area as well as the west side hills of the San Joaquin Valley and the Bakersfield area where there were a few reports of minor wind damage. Widespread gusts of 50 mph or higher were reported in the foothill and mountain areas with a couple of stations near Lebec reporting gusts exceeding 75 mph. After the winds diminished during the afternoon of December 22, widespread precipitation spread across the area with several reports of 1 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation in the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills. Rain shadowing generally kept precipitation totals down in the San Joaquin Valley where most locations picked up between a quarter and half and inch of rainfall. A swath of moderate to locally heavy rainfall was observed in central Tulare County during the evening of December 22 as the main low pressure center tracked north through the area and impacted Porterville, Lindsay and Exeter. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several stations in the higher Sierra picked up between 10 and 15 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Meadows SNOTEL (7600 feet) picked up an estimated 18 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the strong storm which impacted the area and disrupted travel over the previous two days moved into Arizona during the morning of December 27, high pressure moved into the area and brought drier weather, clearing skies and decreased winds. This resulted in a strip of dense fog forming along the middle of the San Joaquin Valley which reduced visibility to below a quarter mile at some locations during the morning of December 27 disrupting some holiday season travel plans. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning which dissipated by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems from the north and northwest, plus a weak tap into atmospheric river moisture, brought rain, mountain snow, and gusty winds to Southern California. Snow levels reached as low as 2000 feet, and brought high impact snow to locations above 4000 feet. Both I-8 through the San Diego Mountains and I-15 through the Cajon Pass were closed due to heavy snowfall. Between December 22-24, the coastal areas received 1-1.5 inches of rainfall, the valleys received 0.50-1 inch, and the mountain areas received up to 1 inch of rainfall. Again on December 26 through 27, coast and valley areas received 1 to 1.5 inches. Mountain areas received up to an inch. There were many water rescues, particularly in the Tijuana River Valley due to individuals driving through flooded roads and getting stuck. The San Diego River at Fashion Valley reached 10.5 feet on December 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Phelan received 13 inches of snow. Victorville, Apple Valley and Hesperia received 2 to 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought some light precipitation to central California during the morning of January 14 as it moved through the area. precipitation amounts were generally light with much of the central Sierra picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level around 8000 feet. Several high elevation SNOTEL locations picked up between 2 and 4 inches of new snowfall. Much of the remainder of the area picked up a quarter inch or less of of rainfall. A secondary system followed during the morning of December 15. Although this second system did not produce much precipitation, it was much colder with snow falling as low as 4000 feet and producing icy conditions in the Pine Mountain Club area where several cars became stuck. The main impact form this second system was a brief period of strong winds across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several locations measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph and some low impact indicator sites measured gusts exceeding 60 mph during the early morning of December 15. The winds diminished by late morning as the system moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high set up over the Great Basin, ushering in gusty Santa Ana winds to Southern California. The highest wind gust was 91 mph at Hellhole Canyon, a wind prone location in the San Diego Mountains. Many other locations had wind gusts in excess of 70 mph. A few tractor trailers were blown over on I-8 in San Diego County. Valley locations also had some gusty winds, with gusts of 25-45 mph, with locally higher gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alpine RAWS reported a peak wind gust of 62 knots at 0852 AM PST on 12/17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a dry November a pattern change would occur the week of November 25th. The first event lead to elevated fire weather concerns over much of interior northern California. It was followed by a fairly impactful and unseasonably cold winter storm. Snow levels were generally forecast to be around 1500 to 2000 feet, with lower snow levels forecast for the Redding area. This system ended up heavily impacting holiday travel, causing chain controls to be in place, in addition to brief closures of Interstate 5 and full closures of Ebbetts Pass/Route 4 in Alpine County, Monitor Pass/Route 89 (Alpine/Mono) and Sonora Pass/Route 108 in Tuolumne and Mono counties due to winter conditions. Towards the end of the storm, Interstate 80 was closed November 29 to 30th for 12 hours from Colfax to the California/Nevada state line. Significant loss to commerce is expected because of this system. This storm ended up bringing two to three feet of snow to the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions brought an extended period of low to zero visibility to Interstate 80 over the Sierra. Blue Canyon Airport ASOS reported visibility less than 1/4 mile in heavy snow Friday evening through early Saturday. I-80 was shutdown 6 pm PST Friday November 29, 2019. Westbound I-80 was opened around 4:30 am and eastbound I-80 was reopened around 5:30 am on Saturday November 30, 2019. Other highways such as Highway 50 and Highway 20 were also intermittently shutdown throughout the night. Travel and commerce across I-80 over the Sierra was impacted for around 12 hours. Loss to commerce is generally projected to be $4.75 million an hour for an I-80 shutdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From November 30 through December 2, 2019, an impactful winter storm tracked over northern California. Snow levels started off around 2,000 to 2,500 feet and were slow to rise throughout the event. The forecast called for 2 to 6 inches of snow at the lower elevations, while the Sierra could once again see 1 to 3 feet of snow. Majority of the heavy snow for the Winter Storm Warning fell from November 30 through December 2nd. Storm total snow will be posted in November and December's storm data. It's worth noting that the ski resorts within NWS Reno's had snow fall totals ranging from 12 to 30 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sierra-at-Tahoe Ski Resort reported 17 inches of new snow accumulation over a period of 24 hours. This report came in the morning of December 2, 2020 around 9:00AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems from the north and northwest, plus a weak tap into atmospheric river moisture, brought rain, mountain snow, and gusty winds to Southern California. Snow levels reached as low as 2000 feet, and brought high impact snow to locations above 4000 feet. Both I-8 through the San Diego Mountains and I-15 through the Cajon Pass were closed due to heavy snowfall. Between December 22-24, the coastal areas received 1-1.5 inches of rainfall, the valleys received 0.50-1 inch, and the mountain areas received up to 1 inch of rainfall. Again on December 26 through 27, coast and valley areas received 1 to 1.5 inches. Mountain areas received up to an inch. There were many water rescues, particularly in the Tijuana River Valley due to individuals driving through flooded roads and getting stuck. The San Diego River at Fashion Valley reached 10.5 feet on December 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snow accumulation on December 26 in the San Bernardino Mountains: Mountain High 30-36 inches, Wrightwood 22-26 inches, Mt. Baldy (base of mountain) 28 inches, Big Bear Snow Summit 18-26 inches, Snow Valley 18-22 inches, Lake Arrowhead 16 inches. I-15 through the Cajon Pass was closed on December 26th due to heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought some light precipitation to central California during the morning of January 14 as it moved through the area. precipitation amounts were generally light with much of the central Sierra picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level around 8000 feet. Several high elevation SNOTEL locations picked up between 2 and 4 inches of new snowfall. Much of the remainder of the area picked up a quarter inch or less of of rainfall. A secondary system followed during the morning of December 15. Although this second system did not produce much precipitation, it was much colder with snow falling as low as 4000 feet and producing icy conditions in the Pine Mountain Club area where several cars became stuck. The main impact form this second system was a brief period of strong winds across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where several locations measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph and some low impact indicator sites measured gusts exceeding 60 mph during the early morning of December 15. The winds diminished by late morning as the system moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and very cold low pressure system dropped southward along the California coast on Christmas Day bringing some light precipitation inland into central California. The system then turned inland and moved through southern California during the evening of December 25 through the morning of December 26 bringing moderate to heavy precipitation to much of Kern County with lighter amounts further north. Between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation fell across much of Kern County with the snow level between 2500 and 3000 feet. Above the snow line, 1 to 2 feet of new snowfall was reported at several stations and the heavy snowfall resulted in the closure of several highways (including Interstate 5 south of Grapevine) for an extended period of time during a period of normally heavy holiday traffic after several vehicle either slid off of the highway or spun out. One truck driver was killed after being stranded along Interstate 5 for several hours. Across the Kern County Deserts and in the San Joaquin Valley from Bakersfield southward, heavy rainfall resulted in several roads being closed for a few hours with several reports of nuisance flooding reported by the Emergency Managers office or by law enforcement. There were also several reports of strong wind gusts and some reports of minor wind damage in the Fort Tejon and Tehachapi areas. Further north precipitation amounts were significantly lower although parts of Kings County picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall. The remainder of the area generally picked up a quarter of an inch or less of liquid precipitation before the system moved to the east of the area during the evening of December 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public posting on Twitter of 12.0 inches of new snowfall measured by the Tehachapi City Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving in from the Gulf of Alaska and drawing in moisture from the tropics combined to bring the first atmospheric river event of the winter season to the Greater Bay Area. This system brought widespread heavy rainfall, roadway flooding, and strong winds to the region. Rare Storm Warnings were issued over the coastal waters where buoys reported wind gusts in excess of 50 mph. Bay Area peaks recorded wind gusts between 60 to 70 mph with gusts along the Monterey Peninsula and Big Sur Coast at 50 to 60 mph. These winds caused downed trees and power outages across the area. Additionally, convection associated wit the main low caused severe flooding in the city of Chualar where homes were flooded. and cars were stranded on a flooded highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mudslide in number 2 lane of CA 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the storm that produced strong winds and widespread precipitation across the area over the previous few days moved out of the area, skies cleared out and winds diminished allowing for radiational cooling to take place over the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts which resulted in areas of fog forming overnight; and reduced visibility below a quarter mile in some locations. Visibility improved after sunrise at most locations as the fog lifted into a low cloud deck which broke up by late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low pressure system, combined with remnant tropical cyclone moisture, brought heavy rain and mountain snow to southern California. Across the area, 1 to 4 inches of rain fell, which was heaviest on the 19th and 20th. Rainfall rates of 0.50 to 1 inch per hour observed which caused impacts to many areas including Valley Center, Alpine and the Mexico border. A waterspout was spotted (photo) just west of San Clemente and Dana Point (LA waters) on the 20th, with another possible waterspout sighting on the 21st. Snow was observed down to 4200 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large boulder in the road on Highway 79 south of Warner Springs around 1800 PST on November 19th due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the storm that produced strong winds and widespread precipitation across the area over the previous few days moved out of the area, skies cleared out and winds diminished allowing for radiational cooling to take place over the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts which resulted in areas of fog forming overnight; and reduced visibility below a quarter mile in some locations. Visibility improved after sunrise at most locations as the fog lifted into a low cloud deck which broke up by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Fresno and Reedley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported at Edwards AFB. California Highway Patrol reported dense fog caused a collision between a semi-truck and a car at the intersection of Garlock rd. and U.S. 395 resulting in minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped southward along the California coast on December 29 and 30. This system did not produce much precipitation inland with a few stations measuring a few hundredths of an inch of liquid precipitation. Upslope flow behind the departing system resulted in low level moisture banking up along the Tehachapi Mountains during the evening of December 30 resulting in some dense fog for a few hours in the Tehachapi area. The fog combined with subfreezing temperatures to produce freezing fog along State Route 58. The fog broke up by late evening as lower levels dried up.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog with visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Tehachapi by the airport AWOS and by California Highway Patrol on SR 58.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northerly low level flow produced upslope low clouds along the south end of the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 31 as there was plenty of low level moisture in place from recent storms. Some of the low level moisture banked up against the mountains in the Tehachapi area resulting in dense fog with visibility below a quarter mile in subfreezing temperatures which also produced black ice on State Route 58. The fog dissipated by late evening as the low level flow turned to the southeast and dried out the lower atmosphere in the Tehachapi area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog with visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Tehachapi.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bear Valley Ski Resort reported 13 inches of new snow over a 24 hour period on December 23. The elevation of the snow report is 8500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system from the north moved into Southern California, bringing rain, mountain snow and gusty winds. The San Bernardino Mountains received a few inches of snow, heaviest overnight on the 30th. Snow Summit received 4-6 inches, Big Bear City received 4 inches, and Big Bear Lake received 2-3 inches. Winds were a big impact with this storm, especially in the mountains and passes. The Cajon Pass (I-15) had numerous big rigs overturned due to strong winds. A fatality occurred on highway 79, where the driver drove the car into a downed tree over the highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sill Hill reported a peak wind gust of 56 knots around 1700 PST on December 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and very cold low pressure system dropped southward along the California coast on Christmas Day bringing some light precipitation inland into central California. The system then turned inland and moved through southern California during the evening of December 25 through the morning of December 26 bringing moderate to heavy precipitation to much of Kern County with lighter amounts further north. Between 1 and 2 inches of liquid precipitation fell across much of Kern County with the snow level between 2500 and 3000 feet. Above the snow line, 1 to 2 feet of new snowfall was reported at several stations and the heavy snowfall resulted in the closure of several highways (including Interstate 5 south of Grapevine) for an extended period of time during a period of normally heavy holiday traffic after several vehicle either slid off of the highway or spun out. One truck driver was killed after being stranded along Interstate 5 for several hours. Across the Kern County Deserts and in the San Joaquin Valley from Bakersfield southward, heavy rainfall resulted in several roads being closed for a few hours with several reports of nuisance flooding reported by the Emergency Managers office or by law enforcement. There were also several reports of strong wind gusts and some reports of minor wind damage in the Fort Tejon and Tehachapi areas. Further north precipitation amounts were significantly lower although parts of Kings County picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall. The remainder of the area generally picked up a quarter of an inch or less of liquid precipitation before the system moved to the east of the area during the evening of December 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Digier Road Hilltop (Lebec 5NW) measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving in from the Gulf of Alaska and drawing in moisture from the tropics combined to bring the first atmospheric river event of the winter season to the Greater Bay Area. This system brought widespread heavy rainfall, roadway flooding, and strong winds to the region. Rare Storm Warnings were issued over the coastal waters where buoys reported wind gusts in excess of 50 mph. Bay Area peaks recorded wind gusts between 60 to 70 mph with gusts along the Monterey Peninsula and Big Sur Coast at 50 to 60 mph. These winds caused downed trees and power outages across the area. Additionally, convection associated wit the main low caused severe flooding in the city of Chualar where homes were flooded. and cars were stranded on a flooded highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (PG&E) on Coast Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carmel River Lagoon rose significantly overnight after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell over the Carmel River Basin. The Monterey OES phoned to say Public Works tried to breach the Lagoon and was unable to do so in time so the surrounding area had to be evacuated due to flooding. Minor Street flooding was observed along 15th, 16th, 17th Ave as well as Carmelo St and Scenic Rd. The unofficial flood stage of the Carmel River Lagoon is 10 ft. The gauge exceeded 10 ft during this time period before the Lagoon was breached and the water level fell dramatically just after 1 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold front that was responsible for the strong wind gusts and areas of blowing dust on November 25 brought a much colder airmass into the area. During the morning of November 26, diminished winds along with clear skies resulted in the first widespread freeze of the season across the San Joaquin Valley where several stations had low temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on November 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on during the morning of November 26 and dropped southward to the Pacific Northwest coast by the afternoon of the 26th. The low continued to drop southward off the California coast during the morning of November 27 spreading widespread precipitation into central California which continued through the late morning of November 28 which being Thanksgiving Day the storm adversely impacted holiday travel as several major roads were impacted. As this system was a cold storm, snow levels were between 2000 and 2500 feet with widespread accumulating snow taking place across the southern Sierra foothills where many locations above 2500 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of snow and the Kern County Deserts where several which picked up between 3 and 8 inches at most locations. Much of the higher Sierra above 7000 feet picked up between 18 and 36 inches of new snow. Most of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch of rain while a few locations which were impacted by thunderstorms picked up close to an inch of rainfall. The most noticeable impacts from this storm were closures of Interstate 5 between Grapevine and Tajon Pass and State Route 58 in the Tehachapi area due to heavy snowfall and several accidents. In addition to the widespread precipitation and low elevation snowfall, thunderstorms produced locally heavier rainfall and small hail across the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and early evenings of both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report in California City (2405 feet) of an estimated 7 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system continued to develop across the desert southwest on November 28th, and it interacted with considerable moisture to produce bands of moderate to heavy rain across the eastern portions of Riverside County during the evening hours. Isolated thunderstorms were embedded in the rain bands, leading to enhanced rain totals. The heavy rain resulted in an episode of flash flooding over far eastern portions of the county, near the lower Colorado River Valley approximately 4 miles north of East Blythe. During the early evening, local fire and rescue reported a water rescue of a vehicle flooded and floating along highway 95. Fortunately there were no reports of injuries to the driver of the vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system developed across the desert southwest on November 28th, and it interacted with deep moisture to produce widespread rain across portions of eastern Riverside County during the evening hours. Isolated thunderstorms were embedded within the rain bands, increasing rain totals. The heavy rain resulted in an episode of flash flooding over far eastern portions of the county, near the lower Colorado River Valley. According to fire department and rescue units, at 1827PST flash flooding 4 miles north of east Blythe resulted in a car becoming flooded and floating near the intersection of 6th Avenue and Highway 95. A water rescue was needed, and the driver was rescued through the roof of the vehicle. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system developing across the desert southwest interacted with significant amounts of moisture to produce widespread rain across portions on Imperial County starting in the afternoon hours on November 28th. Isolated thunderstorms embedded within areas of rain helped to increase rain totals and the heavy rain resulted in an episode of flash flooding on the western side of the Salton Sea. Local law enforcement reported that flash flooding caused the closure of highway 86 about 5 miles southeast of the town of Winona. The road closure occurred during the late afternoon. Instead of a flash flood warning, an areal flood advisory was issued for the area. There were no reports of any accidents due to the flooded roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system still developing over the desert southwest interacted with significant moisture to produce widespread moderate to heavy rain across the western portion of Imperial County during the afternoon hours on November 28th. Isolated thunderstorms were embedded within the areas of rain, locally enhancing rain totals. The heavy rain resulted in an episode of flash flooding on the western side of the Salton Sea. According to local law enforcement, flash flooding resulted in the closure of highway 86 about 5 miles southeast of the town of Winona. Instead of a flash flood warning, an areal flood advisory was in effect at the time of the flooding. There were no reports of accidents due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm caused by a rapidly deepening bomb cyclone brought heavy snow and high winds to the area, creating blizzard-like conditions at times.  The cyclone moved onshore near the Oregon/California border with central pressure near 971 mb and then moved east-northeast through the area.  Numerous accidents and road closures, including Interstate 5, were reported.  Around 8000 customers lost power due to downed power lines.  The surface pressure recorded in Medford was the second lowest ever recorded at that station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer near Yreka reported 8.3 inches of snow as of 27/1000 PST.  |A CoCoRahs observer 0.9WNW Yreka reported 8.2 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 27/0800 PST. Interstate 5 was closed from Siskiyou Summit to Mount Shasta City due to snow and wind combining to make blizzard conditions. The Weed Airport RAWS reported a gust to 63 mph at 26/1448 PST and a gust to 62 mph at 26/2348 PST.   The PacificCorp sensor in North Weed recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 26/1311 PST and 26/2010 PST. The peak gust was 70 mph recorded at 26/1420 PST. The ASOS at Montague Airport recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 26/1757 PST and 26/2021 PST. The peak gust was 67 mph, recorded at 26/1922 PST.

The surface pressure recorded in Medford was the second lowest ever recorded at that station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at the Mount Shasta Ranger Station 1S Mount Shasta City reported 13.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 27/1800 PST.  A cooperative observer 1N Mount Shasta City reported a storm total of 15.0 inches of snow at 27/1400 PST. A spotter 3NW Mount Shasta City reported a storm total of 18.0 inches of snow at 27/1333 PST.  A spotter 3SSE Mount Shasta City reported 12.0 inches of snow at 26/2130 PST.  A spotter 3NW Mount Shasta City reported 16.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 26/2030 PST. Interstate 5 was closed from Siskiyou Summit to Mount Shasta City due to snow and wind combining to make blizzard conditions. Interstate 5 was also closed from Dunsmuir to Mount Shasta City due to heavy snow.

Most of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch of rain while a few locations which were impacted by thunderstorms picked up close to an inch of rainfall. The most noticeable impacts from this storm were closures of Interstate 5 between Grapevine and Tajon Pass and State Route 58 in the Tehachapi area due to heavy snowfall and several accidents. In addition to the widespread precipitation and low elevation snowfall, thunderstorms produced locally heavier rainfall and small hail across the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and early evenings of both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresno Television Station reported a funnel cloud over Dinuba.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and powerful winter storm brought significant winter storm conditions to the Antelope Valley as well as the mountains of Santa Barbara, Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Snow levels bottomed out to between 2000 and 3000 feet. Across the Antelope Valley, snowfall totals of 3 to 7 inches were reported. In the mountains, snowfall totals up to 34 inches were recorded. Additionally, gusty winds in excess of 50 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow blanketed the Antelope Valley as snow levels dropped to around 2000 feet. Weather spotters reported between 3 and 7 inches of snowfall, both in the foothills as well as the valley floor. Significant travel issues were reported, including the closure of Highway 138.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm caused by a rapidly deepening bomb cyclone brought heavy snow and high winds to the area, creating blizzard-like conditions at times.  The cyclone moved onshore near the Oregon/California border with central pressure near 971 mb and then moved east-northeast through the area.  Numerous accidents and road closures, including Interstate 5, were reported.  Around 8000 customers lost power due to downed power lines.  The surface pressure recorded in Medford was the second lowest ever recorded at that station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer at Fort Jones reported 8.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 27/1000 PST.

Due to warm and dry Santa Ana winds as well as critically dry vegetation, the fire continued to burn into early November, eventually burning 9999 acres. Fortunately, no significant damages or injuries were reported.

CZ_NAME: EAST BAY HILLS AND THE DIABLO RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving in from the Gulf of Alaska and drawing in moisture from the tropics combined to bring the first atmospheric river event of the winter season to the Greater Bay Area. This system brought widespread heavy rainfall, roadway flooding, and strong winds to the region. Rare Storm Warnings were issued over the coastal waters where buoys reported wind gusts in excess of 50 mph. Bay Area peaks recorded wind gusts between 60 to 70 mph with gusts along the Monterey Peninsula and Big Sur Coast at 50 to 60 mph. These winds caused downed trees and power outages across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calaveras Road RAWS station recorded gusts between 46 and 56 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fishing boat capsized 30 miles NW of Bodega Bay in the early evening of the 9th. CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four people were crabbing at Doran Beach in Bodega Bay the afternoon of the 25th when their boat capsized in rough waters. All four were wearing life jackets and made it to shore unharmed. At that time, swells were building ahead of a high surf advisory. The Bodega Bay Buoy shows swell heights of 11 feet at 15 seconds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From November 29 through December 2, 2019, an impactful winter storm tracked over northern California. Snow levels started off around 2,000 to 2,500 feet and were slow to rise throughout the event. The forecast called for 2 to 6 inches of snow at the lower elevations, while the Sierra could once again see 1 to 3 feet of snow. This storm ended up having significant travel and loss of commerce impacts, as Interstate 80 was closed November 29 to 30th for 12 hours from Colfax to the California/Nevada state line. Majority of the heavy snow for the Winter Storm Warning fell from November 30 through December 2nd. Storm total snow and other hazards besides this highway closure will be posted in December's storm data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions brought an extended period of low to zero visibility to Interstate 80 over the Sierra. Blue Canyon Airport ASOS reported visibility less than 1/4 mile in heavy snow Friday evening through early Saturday. I-80 was shutdown 6 pm PST Friday November 29, 2019. Westbound I-80 was opened around 4:30 am and eastbound I-80 was reopened around 5:30 am on Saturday November 30, 2019. Other highways such as Highway 50 and Highway 20 were also intermittently shutdown throughout the night. Travel and commerce across I-80 over the Sierra was impacted for around 12 hours. Loss to commerce is generally projected to be $4.75 million an hour for an I-80 shutdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on during the morning of November 26 and dropped southward to the Pacific Northwest coast by the afternoon of the 26th. The low continued to drop southward off the California coast during the morning of November 27 spreading widespread precipitation into central California which continued through the late morning of November 28 which being Thanksgiving Day the storm adversely impacted holiday travel as several major roads were impacted. As this system was a cold storm, snow levels were between 2000 and 2500 feet with widespread accumulating snow taking place across the southern Sierra foothills where many locations above 2500 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of snow and the Kern County Deserts where several which picked up between 3 and 8 inches at most locations. Much of the higher Sierra above 7000 feet picked up between 18 and 36 inches of new snow. Most of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch of rain while a few locations which were impacted by thunderstorms picked up close to an inch of rainfall. The most noticeable impacts from this storm were closures of Interstate 5 between Grapevine and Tajon Pass and State Route 58 in the Tehachapi area due to heavy snowfall and several accidents. In addition to the widespread precipitation and low elevation snowfall, thunderstorms produced locally heavier rainfall and small hail across the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and early evenings of both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gin Flat SNOTEL (7050 feet) picked up an estimated 20 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system interacted with deep moisture to produce widespread moderate to heavy rain across eastern portions of Imperial County on November 28th. Isolated thunderstorms were embedded within the rain bands and they increased rain totals. The widespread rain which mostly occurred during the evening hours led to an episode of flash flooding over the far southeastern portion of the county. At about 1934PST, heavy rain led to flash flooding along Ogilby Road, as reported by the California Highway Patrol. The flash flooding forced the closure of the road and as a result there were no reports of accidents due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system interacted with deep moisture to produce widespread rain across the eastern portion of Imperial County during the evening hours on November 28th. Isolated thunderstorms were embedded within the rain bands, helping to increase rain totals. The heavy rain produced an episode of flash flooding over the far southeast portion of the county. According to the department of highways, at 1934PST flash flooding led to the closure of Ogilby road from Interstate 8 northward to state route 78. Due to the road closure, there were no reports of accidents resulting from the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying and ultimately record setting low pressure system moved into northern California and the Pacific Northwest in late November. A strong cold front associated with this system swept through the Bay Area bringing heavy rain, roadway flooding, strong winds, low elevation snow, small hail, and large waves to the region. This low set a record for the lowest sea-level pressure ever recorded in the state of California near Crescent City of 973.4 mb. Numerous webcams picked up snow on our area peaks down to 2000 feet in elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding at US 101N and 9th St off ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture from tropical cyclone Raymond and a pair of upper level lows led to the development of a zone of slow-moving moderate to heavy rain showers and thunderstorms. A few storms trained over the same areas resulting in high rainfall amounts over a short period, which led to flash flooding with running washes and water over roadways in parts of eastern Riverside and Imperial counties of southeastern California. Flooding and flash flooding continued into the very early morning hours of the 20th across portions of far eastern Imperial County; shortly after midnight flash flooding resulted in the closure of State Route 78 about 12 miles northeast of Glamis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure developing across the desert southwest combined with elevated moisture to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern Riverside County during the late evening hours on November 19th. The resulting heavy rain led to an episode of flash flooding along Highway 62 about 15 miles to the north of the town of Midland. According to a report from the California Highway Patrol, roadway flooding due to running washes occurred at Mile Marker 110 along State Route 62. The report was received via Twitter, at about 2252PST. No accidents were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low pressure center which approached the California coast on November 19 moved inland across southern California on November 20. This system produced widespread precipitation across the area with the most significant precipitation impacting Kern and Tulare Counties. Flooding along State Route 99 in the Wheeler Ridge and Mettler areas resulted in the highway being closed for a period of time. The Grapevine and Frazier Park areas generally picked up between half and inch and an inch of liquid precipitation while many locations in the Tehachapi and Lake Isabella areas and the Kern County Deserts as well as the southern Sierra Nevada in Tulare County picked up between a quarter inch and a half inch of precipitation. The snow level was generally around 6000 feet, but briefly lowered to 5000 feet at times in heavier showers. Most locations in the mountains in Kern and Tulare Counties above 6000 feet picked up between 6 and 9 inches of snow while there were some locally higher accumulations around Pine Mountain Club. Further north, precipitation amounts were much lower with most locations picking up less than a tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding on State Route 99 just north of the intersection with Interstate 5 near Wheeler Ridge. Mud and Debris blocked the northbound lanes and two of the southbound lanes resulting in State Route 99 being closed from the Interstate 5 intersection to Mettler until late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropping southward through the Great Basin region pushed a strong cold front through central California which produced a period of very strong winds along with areas of blowing dust during the late morning and early afternoon hours of November 25. Several stations in the Kern County Deserts and in the Tehachapi and Lake Isabella areas measured gusts exceeding 50 mph while a few locations measured gusts exceeding 70 mph. The strong winds caused at least three semi trucks to be blown over near Mojave and briefly closed State Route 14 due to blowing dust reducing visibility to near zero. In addition, several stations in the San Joaquin Valley measured wind gusts between 35 and 50 mph and blowing dust resulted in a multi-vehicle collision near Caruthers along State Route 41. The winds decreased by early evening as colder air settled into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 78 mph.

A strong cold front associated with this system swept through the Bay Area bringing heavy rain, roadway flooding, strong winds, low elevation snow, small hail, and large waves to the region. This low set a record for the lowest sea-level pressure ever recorded in the state of California near Crescent City of 973.4 mb. Numerous webcams picked up snow on our area peaks down to 2000 feet in elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (PGE) in Carmel Valley Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropping southward through the Great Basin region pushed a strong cold front through central California which produced a period of very strong winds along with areas of blowing dust during the late morning and early afternoon hours of November 25. Several stations in the Kern County Deserts and in the Tehachapi and Lake Isabella areas measured gusts exceeding 50 mph while a few locations measured gusts exceeding 70 mph. The strong winds caused at least three semi trucks to be blown over near Mojave and briefly closed State Route 14 due to blowing dust reducing visibility to near zero. In addition, several stations in the San Joaquin Valley measured wind gusts between 35 and 50 mph and blowing dust resulted in a multi-vehicle collision near Caruthers along State Route 41. The winds decreased by early evening as colder air settled into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on during the morning of November 26 and dropped southward to the Pacific Northwest coast by the afternoon of the 26th. The low continued to drop southward off the California coast during the morning of November 27 spreading widespread precipitation into central California which continued through the late morning of November 28 which being Thanksgiving Day the storm adversely impacted holiday travel as several major roads were impacted. As this system was a cold storm, snow levels were between 2000 and 2500 feet with widespread accumulating snow taking place across the southern Sierra foothills where many locations above 2500 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of snow and the Kern County Deserts where several which picked up between 3 and 8 inches at most locations. Much of the higher Sierra above 7000 feet picked up between 18 and 36 inches of new snow. Most of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch of rain while a few locations which were impacted by thunderstorms picked up close to an inch of rainfall. The most noticeable impacts from this storm were closures of Interstate 5 between Grapevine and Tajon Pass and State Route 58 in the Tehachapi area due to heavy snowfall and several accidents. In addition to the widespread precipitation and low elevation snowfall, thunderstorms produced locally heavier rainfall and small hail across the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and early evenings of both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troy Meadow SNOTEL (7800 feet) picked up an estimated 26 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and wet Pacific low pressure system centered west of Arizona led to widespread moderate to heavy showers along with embedded isolated thunderstorms over portions of far southeast California on November 28th. The evening rain was sufficient to cause episodes of flash flooding which persisted past midnight and into the early morning hours on November 29th. The California Highway Patrol reported that flash flooding across far eastern Riverside county caused flooding at State Route 78 and Seeley Road shortly after midnight. A Flash Flood Warning was in effect at the time and there were no reports of accidents due to the flooded roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific upper low centered west of Arizona led to widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms across portions of far southeast California, including far eastern Riverside County, during the evening hours on the 28th. The heavy rain resulted in some flash flooding which persisted into the early morning hours on November 29th. According to a report from the California Highway Patrol, flash flooding 4 miles southwest of Blythe resulted in roadway flooding at the intersection of State Route 78 and Seeley Road. The flooding occurred at roughly 0042PST. A Flash Flood Warning was in effect at the time of the flooding and fortunately, there were no reports of accidents due to the flooded roads.

The strong winds caused at least three semi trucks to be blown over near Mojave and briefly closed State Route 14 due to blowing dust reducing visibility to near zero. In addition, several stations in the San Joaquin Valley measured wind gusts between 35 and 50 mph and blowing dust resulted in a multi-vehicle collision near Caruthers along State Route 41. The winds decreased by early evening as colder air settled into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a semi truck blown over on exit 167 on State Route 58 at the State Route 14 exchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on during the morning of November 26 and dropped southward to the Pacific Northwest coast by the afternoon of the 26th. The low continued to drop southward off the California coast during the morning of November 27 spreading widespread precipitation into central California which continued through the late morning of November 28 which being Thanksgiving Day the storm adversely impacted holiday travel as several major roads were impacted. As this system was a cold storm, snow levels were between 2000 and 2500 feet with widespread accumulating snow taking place across the southern Sierra foothills where many locations above 2500 feet picked up between 5 and 10 inches of snow and the Kern County Deserts where several which picked up between 3 and 8 inches at most locations. Much of the higher Sierra above 7000 feet picked up between 18 and 36 inches of new snow. Most of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and half an inch of rain while a few locations which were impacted by thunderstorms picked up close to an inch of rainfall. The most noticeable impacts from this storm were closures of Interstate 5 between Grapevine and Tajon Pass and State Route 58 in the Tehachapi area due to heavy snowfall and several accidents. In addition to the widespread precipitation and low elevation snowfall, thunderstorms produced locally heavier rainfall and small hail across the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and early evenings of both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report in Bear Valley Springs (5700 feet) of an estimated 11 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some active weather, skies began to clear and temperatures dropped. Cold overnight lows affected the region for a few days at the end of November. A record low maximum temperature was also broken in San Francisco on Thanksgiving day with a high temperature of 48 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving in from the Gulf of Alaska and drawing in moisture from the tropics combined to bring the first atmospheric river event of the winter season to the Greater Bay Area. This system brought widespread heavy rainfall, roadway flooding, and strong winds to the region. Rare Storm Warnings were issued over the coastal waters where buoys reported wind gusts in excess of 50 mph. Bay Area peaks recorded wind gusts between 60 to 70 mph with gusts along the Monterey Peninsula and Big Sur Coast at 50 to 60 mph. These winds caused downed trees and power outages across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station in Santa Rita recorded 0.41 inches of rain. Timing is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kincade Fire was sparked in the evening of October 23rd during the first of a series of offshore wind events. The second event in late October promoted the rapid spread of the Kincade Fire across portions of Sonoma County. Much of the county was evacuated during this time and wind gusts were recorded as high as 102 mph in the vicinity of the fire. The fire continued to burn into early November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kincade Fire broke out on the night of Oct 23rd. The fire was put out on the evening of November 6th. In all, the fire burned 77,758 acres, 374 structures were destroyed, 60 were damaged, and 4 first responders were injured with one injury deemed serious https://www.fire.ca.gov/incidents/2019/10/23/kincade-fire/. The fire prompted numerous emergency evacuations throughout Sonoma County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first 18 days of November were noted for being unusually dry across the area as a large area of dry high pressure dominated the weather picture. Temperatures were above normal across the area from November 3 onward. Finally a strong low pressure center approached the southern California coast on November 19 bringing increased winds to the area. Several locations along the west side hills of the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County Mountains and Deserts reported wind gusts exceeding 30 mph. A few low impact indicator sites near Tehachapi and Lake Isabella briefly had gusts above 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northern California experienced seasonably warm temperatures the weekend of July 12, 2019 due to high pressure sitting over the area. Rivers during this time were still fast and cold moving in the foothills and mountains with water temperatures spanning 48 to 60 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening on July 13, around 1700 PST, a young girl fell into the South Yuba River where it intersects with Highway 49. The father of the child jumped in after her and then went missing around 1740 PST. State park officials were able to rescue the daughter; however, they were not able to find the father as he was swept away down stream. Ultimately his body was recovered on July 21, 2019 at 1145 PST about a half mile downstream from the Highway 49 bridge. The news article noted that the Sheriff's office said that the South Yuba River was flowing at four to five times normal because of all the snow melt.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On July 17, 2019 the Plumas County Sheriff's Office posted on their Facebook page about a cold water death that occurred near the Rock Creek Bridge on Highway 70 in the Feather River Canyon. The Sheriff's Office received the call at 1434PST that a victim had fallen into the water while hiking and at the time of call they were still submerged underwater. Efforts were made by the victim���s husband to pull her out of the rapids but the current was too strong. The victim's body was recovered at 1744PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tick Fire burned over 4600 acres in the Canyon County area of Los Angeles county. The combination of warm and dry Santa Ana winds as well as critically dry vegetation allowed for significant fire growth. The fire destroyed and damaged numerous residences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tick Fire burned 4615 acres in the Canyon County area of Los Angeles county. The combination of warm and dry Santa Ana winds and critically dry vegetation allowed for significant fire growth. The fire destroyed 23 homes and damaged 40 other residences. During the incident, four firefighter injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low tracked over southern Oregon and northern California from August 09 through August 11. A series of shortwave troughs brought showers and thunderstorms to the region which produced damaging winds, severe hail, and abundant lightning. Law enforcement reported many large tree branches, up to the size of a car at times, blocking roadways including state route 299. Wind gusts appear to be sub-severe, between 25-35 mph, and were estimated via radar, observations, and the unrestricted mesoscale analysis (URMA). Additionally, this system was responsible for starting multiple wildfires by lightning, but most fires were under 0.5 acres and fire crews were able to extinguish or control these fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Friday, August 9, roughly 7 miles to the northwest of Red Bluff, a strong thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail stones that fell for 5 to 10 minutes. The public reported that they noticed some minor damage to trees and plants in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure dropped into eastern California from the west-northwest on the 13th and produced rain and high elevation snow across northeast California and the Lake Tahoe Basin starting the morning of the 13th. Locations in the Lake Tahoe Basin received 0.25 inches to 1.50 inches of liquid, with the west side receiving the highest amounts. Higher elevations within the Tahoe Basin, mainly above 8000 feet, reported snow totals ranging from 2 to 15 inches. Only trace amounts of rain were reported across Mono County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alpine Meadows Ski Resort reported 15 inches of snowfall at an elevation of 6950 feet MSL and 22 inches at an elevation of 7880 feet MSL in a 48-hour period from early on the 13th through the morning of the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high situated over New Mexico and Texas brought hot weather to the inland areas of Southern California. The hottest location was the Coachella Valley, where Thermal reached 117 degrees, and Palm Springs reached 116 degrees on August 21st. The San Diego Deserts were just as hot, with Ocotillo Wells reaching 117 degrees on August 21st. Up north in the High Desert, many locations were over 100 degrees, such as Apple Valley and Hesperia, where the high was 103 for both locations on August 21st. A few locations in the Inland Empire reached over 100 degrees on August 21st, such as Hemet (104 degrees) and Lake Elsinore (103 degrees). The San Diego Valleys also had hot weather, with many locations reaching 90-95 degrees and Ramona reaching 100 degrees on August 21st. The month of August was dry for all deserts and mountains making it one of the driest on record (under 10 percent of average).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many locations throughout the Coachella Valley experienced excessive heat on August 21st. Thermal reached 117 degrees and Palm Springs reached 116 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Critical fire weather conditions were forecast for Oct 23-24 and again on October 27-28 with the eastern Pacific High allowing trough to slide into the Great Basin/Nevada areas. This caused the pressure gradients to tighten over the county warning area, which lead to additional rounds of strong northerly and easterly winds. With these winds forecast, dry conditions still in place, and fuels still being primed a Red Flag Warning was issued for most of interior northern California. Fortunately no major wildfires resulted from this but utility power lines did suffer from wind and tree damage across much of northern California. Time stamps for events are based on strongest wind gusts that occurred between Oct 23-38.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station, Hell Hole, near Big Bend Summit, reported a non-thunderstorm related wind gust up to 68 mph. Throughout El Dorado, Nevada, Yuba, and Placer counties, other mesonet stations reported peak wind gusts of 30-68 mph throughout the day. The local utility company for these counties reported tree and wind related damage to power lines near the Bonnie Nook, Weimar, Brunswick, Columbia Hill, Grass Valley, Higgins, Deer Creek/Drum, Colgate/Alleghany, Mountain Quarries, Placerville, and Challenge areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the lower deserts of Imperial County - from the Imperial Valley eastward to the lower Colorado River valley - during the afternoon and evening hours on September 25th. Instability was elevated due to the close proximity of an upper level low pressure system and cool air aloft, and the increased instability aided the thunderstorm development. As was the case over the past couple of days, the stronger thunderstorms primarily generated very heavy rain with peak rain rates well in excess of one inch per hour. Multiple reports from the California Highway Patrol indicated that flash flooding was occurring along State Route 78, from near the Cibola National Wildlife Refuge south to near Interstate 8. Local law enforcement also reported 30 vehicles stuck due to flowing water in a wash near Ogilby Road about 10 miles northeast of Glamis. Later in the evening, around 1945PST, there was a public report of flash flooding in Brawley where water at least one foot deep was overflowing an embankment. Multiple Flash Flood Warnings were issued; fortunately there were no reports of injuries due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the eastern portion of Imperial County during the afternoon hours on September 25th. The stronger storms generated heavy rainfall with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain resulted in some flash flooding that affected motorists on Ogilby Road to the south of State Route 78. According to the California Highway Patrol, at 1632PST flash flooding resulted in a vehicle being washed out along Ogilby Road just south of State Route 78, and about 10 miles east-northeast of the town of Glamis. The driver was not injured and was helped out of the vehicle. Additionally, 30 more vehicles were also stopped and stuck before a flowing wash in the vicinity. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued at 1436PST and was in effect at the time of the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the eastern portion of Imperial County during the afternoon hours on September 25th. Some of the stronger storms produced heavy rain, with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain resulted in flash flooding along State Route 78 to the south-southwest of the town of Palo Verde. According to a report from the California Highway Patrol, at 1509PST flash flooding 10 miles southwest of Palo Verde forced the closure of State Route 78. The road was closed so that Caltrans would be able to clear debris off of the road. Flash flooding allowed water to rise 2.5 feet above the road at the Milpitas Wash Road.  Additionally, a bit earlier at 1427PST the California Highway Patrol indicated that State Route 78 was closed near Mile Marker 71 due to moderate flash flooding. There were no reports of accidents or injuries due to the flash flooding. A Flash Flood Warning was issued at 1500PST for areas just east of the flooding, and a second warning was put out at 1604PST that covered areas affected by the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inside slider upper low brought moisture and instability to SoCal on April 22nd. Thunderstorms developed over the mountains and deserts. Heavy rain, thunder and lightning occurred in these storms. A waterspout was observed in Morena Lake and a webcam captured a possible funnel cloud over Mount Laguna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KUSI TV shared several photos of a waterspout over Lake Morena. Early a report and photo of a funnel cloud was capture on SDGE webcamera near Mt Laguna. No damage reported but water spray evident over the lake. Thunderstorms and cumulus clouds were in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over the Great Basin brought heat, high winds and low relative humidity to Southern California. Many locations in the Inland Empire reached 100-105 degrees on the 13th. Orange and San Diego County valleys reached 90-99 degrees on the 13th. The Coachella Valley saw the highest temperatures in the region with highs topping out around 110F on Friday. Similar high temperatures occurred on the 14th. A weak Santa Ana event occurred with peak wind gusts ranging from 20-40 mph, highest in the mountains. Numerous small wildfires broke out due to the low humidity and gusty winds. One of the largest fires was the Horseshoe Fire near Hemet. It prompted evacuations of a small community near North Warren Road. No injuries or damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coachella Valley experienced hot temperatures on September 13th and 14th. Palm Springs Airport and Thermal reached 110 degrees on September 13th. Similar temperatures were observed on the 14th.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure centered over the Four Corners ushered in moisture from the southeast into SoCal starting on Labor Day. Monsoon thunderstorms impacted the mountains and deserts September 2 through 5th. The event started with a nocturnal MCS over the Coachella Valley early in the morning on September 2nd, dumping 1-1.5 inches over a couple of hours. Even more thunderstorms formed Monday afternoon, especially in the mountains. Hail was reported on I-8 Monday afternoon. Storms continued each afternoon and evening in the mountains through September 5th. Some thunderstorms even reached the San Diego County coast on September 4th. Widespread outflow wind gusts of 30-45 mph were reported on the coast and in the valleys on September 4th.|At the end of September, 24-26, tropical moisture was wrapped into southern California from a closed upper low. This initially brought Santa Ana winds and very warm temperatures to the coast and on 25-26 brought scattered thunderstorms to the San Diego mountains. The flooding rains were confined to the Mojave Desert and Imperial Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tenaja Fire started on September 4th in the town of La Cresta in Riverside County and quickly grew to over 1,900 acres over the course of two days. 1,200 people were evacuated, and many schools closed due to the poor air quality. Two structures were damaged and three firefighters suffered minor injuries. The cause of the fire is still under investigation, but lightning may be the cause. The fire was contained on September 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure led to hot and dry weather over Labor Day weekend. Local waters ways were still running cold around 60 to 65 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple news outlets reported that a 41 year old man had either fallen or jumped off a boat on the evening of September 2. A music event was on going at this time, but given that the water was dark and murky, the man was unable to be retrieved. Local law enforcement was able to recover the man's body about 45 minutes after he fell in and tried to resuscitate him, but failed. The corner ruled the man's death a drowning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure centered over the Four Corners ushered in moisture from the southeast into SoCal starting on Labor Day. Monsoon thunderstorms impacted the mountains and deserts September 2 through 5th. The event started with a nocturnal MCS over the Coachella Valley early in the morning on September 2nd, dumping 1-1.5 inches over a couple of hours. Even more thunderstorms formed Monday afternoon, especially in the mountains. Hail was reported on I-8 Monday afternoon. Storms continued each afternoon and evening in the mountains through September 5th. Some thunderstorms even reached the San Diego County coast on September 4th. Widespread outflow wind gusts of 30-45 mph were reported on the coast and in the valleys on September 4th.|At the end of September, 24-26, tropical moisture was wrapped into southern California from a closed upper low. This initially brought Santa Ana winds and very warm temperatures to the coast and on 25-26 brought scattered thunderstorms to the San Diego mountains. The flooding rains were confined to the Mojave Desert and Imperial Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported several basketball-sized boulders blocking lanes on highway 78 near Banner.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure over the Great Basin brought heat, high winds and low relative humidity to Southern California. Many locations in the Inland Empire reached 100-105 degrees on the 13th. Orange and San Diego County valleys reached 90-99 degrees on the 13th. The Coachella Valley saw the highest temperatures in the region with highs topping out around 110F on Friday. Similar high temperatures occurred on the 14th. A weak Santa Ana event occurred with peak wind gusts ranging from 20-40 mph, highest in the mountains. Numerous small wildfires broke out due to the low humidity and gusty winds. One of the largest fires was the Horseshoe Fire near Hemet. It prompted evacuations of a small community near North Warren Road. No injuries or damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horseshoe Fire broke out around 1700 PST on September 14th in Riverside County. 60 homes were evacuated in a small community. The fire was 520 acres and was fully contained on September 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated monsoon thunderstorms developed over portions of eastern Imperial County during the afternoon hours on July 23rd. The stronger storms generated locally heavy rains with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain led to one episode of flash flooding over rather open country in far eastern Imperial County. At about 1600MST the California Highway Patrol reported a flash flood event on Ogilby Road. They reported that low clearance vehicles would not be able to make it across flooded portions of the road and as a result the road would be closed until further notice. No reports of accidents were received. A Flash Flood Warning was not issued in this case.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated monsoon thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Imperial County during the afternoon hours on July 23rd. The stronger storms produced locally heavy rains with peak rain rates in excess of one inch per hour; the heavy rain was sufficient to cause one episode of flash flooding across the far eastern portion of the county. According to the California Highway Patrol, at about 1600MST flash flooding occurred along Ogilby Road, about 11 miles northwest of Felicity. The highway patrol indicated that low clearance vehicles would not be able to make it across flooded portions of the road and as a result the road would be closed until further notice. A Flash Flood Warning was issued at 1719PST, after the report of the flooding. No accidents were reported due to the flooding.
